blocks.x: Percent means per hundred, from the Latin per centum. So \(73\%\) is literally \(\frac{73}{100}\), a fraction with a hidden denominator of \(100\). The little \(\%\) sign is just standing in for that buried \(/100\).

blocks.cap: The word percent just means per hundred, so the whole grid is split into exactly \(100\) equal squares and \(73\) of them are filled. That shaded amount is \(73\%\), which is the same as saying \(73\) out of \(100\), the fraction \(\tfrac{73}{100}\). And since a fraction over \(100\) reads straight off as a decimal, that very same shaded patch is also \(0.73\). One amount, three names, \(73\%\) and \(\tfrac{73}{100}\) and \(0.73\), and the rest of this lesson is just learning to slide between them.

blocks.x: Your phone storage is \(80\%\) full. Write \(80\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: The word percent means "per hundred," so put the number over 100. That gives you \(\tfrac{80}{100}\), and now it is just a fraction waiting to be simplified.
Both 80 and 100 are divisible by 20. Divide the top by 20 and the bottom by 20 to land on the smallest equivalent fraction.
blocks.sol: Start by reading the percent literally. \(80\%\) means 80 per hundred, so write it over 100. $$80\% = \frac{80}{100}$$ Now reduce, just like any fraction. The greatest common factor of 80 and 100 is 20, so divide both the top and the bottom by 20. $$\frac{80}{100} = \frac{80 \div 20}{100 \div 20} = \frac{4}{5}$$ A quick check, \(\tfrac{4}{5}\) of the storage is the same slice as \(80\%\) of it, just written in lowest terms. So the answer is \(\boxed{4/5}\).

blocks.cap: A percent slides straight onto a denominator of \(100\), so \(80\%\) becomes \(\tfrac{80}{100}\). From there it reduces like any other fraction, dividing top and bottom by \(20\) to land on \(\tfrac{4}{5}\). The same move turns \(36\%\) into \(\tfrac{36}{100}\), and dividing by \(4\) gives \(\tfrac{9}{25}\).

blocks.x: A bakery sold \(250\%\) as many sourdough loaves as last Saturday. Percents can exceed \(100\). Write \(250\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: A percent means that many hundredths, so \(250\%\) is the same as \(\tfrac{250}{100}\). Going above \(100\) is allowed, it simply gives a fraction larger than \(1\).
Now reduce \(\tfrac{250}{100}\). Both the top and bottom share a factor of \(50\), so divide each by \(50\).
blocks.sol: A percent is a number of hundredths, so I put the percent over \(100\). $$250\% = \frac{250}{100}$$ Going past \(100\) is fine, it just means the fraction is bigger than \(1\). Now I reduce. The greatest common factor of \(250\) and \(100\) is \(50\), so I divide top and bottom by \(50\). $$\frac{250}{100} = \frac{250 \div 50}{100 \div 50} = \frac{5}{2}$$ That matches the "two and a half times" idea, since \(\tfrac{5}{2}\) is \(2.5\). So \(250\%\) in lowest terms is \(\boxed{5/2}\).

blocks.x: A glacier retreated by \(-45\%\) over a decade. A percent can be negative. Write \(-45\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Start from the meaning. \(-45\%\) means \(-45\) out of \(100\), so write it as \(\tfrac{-45}{100}\). The negative sign just rides along on top.
Now reduce \(\tfrac{-45}{100}\). Both \(45\) and \(100\) share a factor of \(5\), so divide each by \(5\) and keep the minus sign.
blocks.sol: The percent sign means "per hundred," so put the number over \(100\) and let the minus sign stay with it. That gives \(-45\% = \tfrac{-45}{100}\). Now simplify. Both \(45\) and \(100\) are divisible by \(5\), so divide the top and bottom by \(5\). The top becomes \(-45 \div 5 = -9\) and the bottom becomes \(100 \div 5 = 20\), so \(\tfrac{-45}{100} = \tfrac{-9}{20}\). Since \(9\) and \(20\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\), this is lowest terms. \(\boxed{-9/20}\)

blocks.x: Percent means per hundred. The word comes from the Latin per centum, so \(x\%\) is just a shorter name for the fraction \(\frac{x}{100}\), with the \(\%\) sign standing in for the hidden denominator. To turn a percent into a fraction, write it over \(100\) and divide the top and bottom by their greatest common factor until nothing else divides both. Nothing traps the numerator between \(0\) and \(100\): more than one whole shows up as more than \(100\%\), so \(250\% = \frac{250}{100} = \frac{5}{2}\), and it can even go negative. The \(100\) was never a wall, only the denominator underneath.
A coupon takes \(38\%\) off. Rewrite \(38\%\) as a decimal.
hints: A percent is a count per hundred, so \(38\%=\tfrac{38}{100}=38\div 100\). You are not taking the percent of any price yet, just rewriting the number itself.
Dividing by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the left. Picture \(38\) as \(38.\) and hop that point left twice, filling the empty spot with a zero.
blocks.sol: Start from the meaning of a percent. \(38\%=\dfrac{38}{100}=38\div 100\). Dividing by \(100\) moves the decimal point two places to the left. Begin at \(38.\), hop the point left once to get \(3.8\), then left again to land at \(0.38\), writing a zero in the now empty tenths-and-ones gap. So \(38\%=\boxed{0.38}\).

blocks.cap: Dividing by \(100\) walks the decimal point two steps to the left. So \(38\%\) becomes \(0.38\), and \(6\%\) becomes \(0.06\), where a placeholder zero fills the tenths spot that the missing digit leaves empty.

blocks.x: A bridge sensor drifts by \(0.4\%\). Write \(0.4\%\) as a decimal.
hints: A percent means "divided by one hundred", so dividing by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the left. Start from \(0.4\) and move the point.
Moving the point in \(0.4\) two places left runs out of digits, so fill the empty spots with zeros. You get \(0.4 \to 0.04 \to 0.004\).
blocks.sol: A percent is a count out of one hundred, so \(0.4\%\) means \(\tfrac{0.4}{100}\). Dividing by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the left. Starting at \(0.4\), one slide gives \(0.04\) and a second slide gives \(0.004\), with zeros filling the empty places. So \(0.4\% = \boxed{0.004}\).

blocks.x: Your fitness app shows daily goal progress as the decimal \(0.41\). Write \(0.41\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: A percent is a count out of one hundred, so to name a decimal as a percent you multiply it by \(100\). Multiplying by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the right.
Slide the point in \(0.41\) two places right. It moves past the \(4\) and past the \(1\), landing after the \(1\), which leaves \(41\).
blocks.sol: A decimal becomes a percent when you multiply it by \(100\), and multiplying by \(100\) just slides the decimal point two places to the right. Start with \(0.41\). Slide the point past the \(4\) to get \(4.1\), then past the \(1\) to get \(41\). So \(0.41 \times 100 = 41\), which means \(0.41 = 41\%\). The answer is \(\boxed{41}\).

blocks.x: A race lap timer reads \(0.073\) of the way through the final lap. Write \(0.073\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: To go from a decimal to a percent, multiply by \(100\), which slides the decimal point two places to the right. Start by moving it from \(0.073\) one place, then a second place.
Sliding \(0.073\) two places right gives \(007.3\). Drop the leading zeros and you are left with a number that still has a digit after the point.
blocks.sol: A percent means how many hundredths, so to convert a decimal to a percent you multiply by \(100\). Multiplying by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the right. Starting from \(0.073\), one slide gives \(0.73\) and a second slide gives \(7.3\). Notice there were three digits after the point, so only two of them clear the point and the last one stays behind in the tenths place. That is why the percent is itself a decimal. So \(0.073 = 7.3\%\), and the number you type is \(\boxed{7.3}\).

blocks.x: Percent \(\leftrightarrow\) decimal. To turn a percent into a decimal, divide by \(100\), which slides the point two places to the left, padding with zeros when you run out of digits, so \(38\% = 0.38\) and \(6\% = 0.06\). To go back, multiply by \(100\), sliding the point two places to the right. Same shift, opposite directions, so the two moves undo each other. If you forget which way, lean on the meaning, since \(38\%\) is \(38\) out of \(100\), which is \(0.38\).
The \(\%\) sign is a frozen abbreviation: Italian merchants wrote per c., which blurred across centuries into the slashed pair of zeros used today. The cousin sign ‰ means per thousand.
A garden bed is \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) planted. A percent has a hidden denominator of \(100\). Write \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: You want the same fraction with a denominator of \(100\). Ask yourself what \(4\) must be multiplied by to reach \(100\), and remember to multiply the top by that same number.
Since \(4 \times 25 = 100\), multiply top and bottom by \(25\). That gives \(\tfrac{3 \times 25}{4 \times 25} = \tfrac{75}{100}\), and the numerator over \(100\) is the percent.
blocks.sol: Rewrite \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) as an equal fraction whose denominator is \(100\). Since \(4 \times 25 = 100\), multiply the top and bottom by the same \(25\). $$\frac{3}{4} = \frac{3 \times 25}{4 \times 25} = \frac{75}{100}.$$ A fraction over \(100\) is read directly as a percent, so \(\tfrac{75}{100} = 75\%\). The percent is \(\boxed{75}\).

blocks.cap: Three corners, one value. Walk the left side and \(75\%\) becomes \(\tfrac{75}{100}=\tfrac{3}{4}\), then back up by rewriting with denominator 100. Walk the right side and \(75\%\) becomes \(0.75\) by sliding the point two places left, then back up by sliding it two places right. Walk the bottom and \(\tfrac{3}{4}=3\div 4=0.75\). A percent, a fraction, and a decimal are three forms of the same value, and these arrows carry you between any two.

blocks.x: A bakery scales a recipe to \(\tfrac{8}{5}\) of the original. Since \(\tfrac{8}{5} > 1\), the percent lands above \(100\). Write \(\tfrac{8}{5}\) as a percent. Answer with just the number.
hints: To get a denominator of \(100\), ask what turns \(5\) into \(100\). Since \(5\times 20=100\), multiply both the top and the bottom by \(20\).
Multiplying gives \(\tfrac{8\times 20}{5\times 20}=\tfrac{160}{100}\). The numerator over \(100\) is the percent.
blocks.sol: A percent is a count out of \(100\), so rewrite \(\tfrac{8}{5}\) with a denominator of \(100\). Since \(5\times 20=100\), multiply the top and bottom by \(20\). $$\frac{8}{5}=\frac{8\times 20}{5\times 20}=\frac{160}{100}.$$ A fraction over \(100\) reads straight off as a percent, so \(\tfrac{160}{100}=160\%\). You can check this by multiplying instead, \(\tfrac{8}{5}\times 100=\tfrac{800}{5}=160\). Because \(\tfrac{8}{5}\) is bigger than one whole, the percent climbed past \(100\), exactly as expected. \(\boxed{160}\)

blocks.x: A recipe calls for \(\tfrac{9}{8}\) cups of oats. Since eighths do not divide \(100\) evenly, multiply by \(100\) and simplify. Write \(\tfrac{9}{8}\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: To turn a fraction into a percent, multiply it by \(100\). So you want \(\tfrac{9}{8}\times 100\), which is the same as \(\tfrac{900}{8}\).
Now simplify \(\tfrac{900}{8}\) by dividing top and bottom by \(4\) to get \(\tfrac{225}{2}\), then divide \(225\) by \(2\). It does not come out even, so expect a decimal ending in a half.
blocks.sol: A fraction becomes a percent when you multiply it by \(100\), so start with \(\tfrac{9}{8}\times 100\). Writing \(100\) over the eight gives $$\frac{9}{8}\times 100=\frac{9\times 100}{8}=\frac{900}{8}.$$ Divide the top and bottom by \(4\) to get \(\tfrac{225}{2}\), and dividing \(225\) by \(2\) gives \(112.5\). The result is more than \(100\) because \(\tfrac{9}{8}\) is more than one whole, and it carries a \(.5\) because halving an odd number leaves a half. So as a percent it is \(\boxed{112.5}\), meaning \(112.5\%\) of a single scoop.

blocks.x: Fraction to percent. Rescale the fraction until its denominator is \(100\), and the numerator is then the percent, since percent already means out of one hundred. This lands cleanly whenever the denominator divides \(100\), as with \(2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 50,\) and \(100\), so \(\frac{4}{5} = \frac{80}{100} = 80\%\). When the denominator does not divide \(100\) neatly, multiply the whole fraction by \(100\) instead, the same measuring-against-a-hundred that works for any fraction at all, so \(\frac{9}{8} \times 100 = 112.5\%\).

Three costumes, one number. A percent, a fraction, and a decimal are three names for the same value, so you can move between them whenever you like, sliding the point two places to swap a decimal and a percent, or going over \(100\) to swap a percent and a fraction. The real payoff is comparing. When two values arrive in different forms your eyes cannot judge which is bigger, so put them both in the same form first, usually all decimals or all over \(100\), and then the larger one is obvious.
Room A chose pizza at \(65\%\). Room B chose pizza at \(\tfrac{3}{5}\). Convert both to decimals and pick the larger share. Type \(1\) for Room A or \(2\) for Room B.
hints: You cannot compare a percent and a fraction while they look different. Turn both into decimals first. For the percent, slide the point two places left. For the fraction, divide the top by the bottom.
Room A is \(65\% = 0.65\). Room B is \(\tfrac{3}{5} = 0.60\). Now the costumes match, so just compare \(0.65\) with \(0.60\) and name the option that is bigger.
blocks.sol: Put both shares in the same form, decimals. Room A is \(65\%\). To turn a percent into a decimal, divide by \(100\), which slides the point two places left, so \(65\% = 0.65\). Room B is \(\tfrac{3}{5}\). Dividing \(3\) by \(5\) gives \(0.60\), and you can check it by rewriting \(\tfrac{3}{5} = \tfrac{6}{10} = 0.60\). Now both are decimals, so compare. Since \(0.65 > 0.60\), Room A has the larger pizza share, which is option 1. \(\boxed{1}\)

blocks.x: A sales tax is \(12.5\%\). Write \(12.5\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Percent means per hundred, so start by writing \(12.5\%\) as \(\tfrac{12.5}{100}\). A fraction in lowest terms cannot keep a decimal upstairs, so you need to clear that half.
Multiply the top and bottom by \(10\) to get rid of the decimal point. That turns \(\tfrac{12.5}{100}\) into \(\tfrac{125}{1000}\), and now both numbers are whole and ready to simplify.
blocks.sol: Percent means per hundred, so \(12.5\%\) becomes \(\tfrac{12.5}{100}\). A tidy fraction has whole numbers top and bottom, so multiply both by \(10\) to slide the decimal away. That gives $$\frac{12.5}{100} = \frac{12.5 \times 10}{100 \times 10} = \frac{125}{1000}.$$ Now reduce. Both \(125\) and \(1{,}000\) share the factor \(125\), and dividing each by it gives \(\tfrac{1}{8}\). So twelve and a half percent is exactly one eighth, which makes sense since \(\tfrac{1}{8}\) of a hundred is \(12.5\). \(\boxed{1/8}\)

review.x: A survey found \(55\%\) of students bike to school. Write \(55\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: A percent is a fraction with a denominator of \(100\), so start by writing \(55\%\) as \(\tfrac{55}{100}\).
Both \(55\) and \(100\) share a factor of \(5\). Divide the top and the bottom by \(5\) to reach lowest terms.
review.sol: Percent means "per hundred," so \(55\%\) is \(55\) out of \(100\). $$55\% = \frac{55}{100}$$ Now reduce. The numerator and denominator both share a factor of \(5\), so divide each by \(5\). $$\frac{55}{100} = \frac{55 \div 5}{100 \div 5} = \frac{11}{20}$$ Since \(11\) is prime and does not divide \(20\), the fraction is in lowest terms. \(\boxed{11/20}\)

review.x: A pollster reports \(24\%\) of people surveyed had never tasted dark chocolate. Write \(24\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: A percent is a count out of one hundred, so start by writing \(24\%\) as \(\tfrac{24}{100}\).
Both \(24\) and \(100\) are divisible by \(4\). Divide the top and the bottom by \(4\) and check that nothing else divides both.
review.sol: The word percent means per hundred, so \(24\%\) is \(24\) out of \(100\). $$24\% = \frac{24}{100}$$ Now simplify. The greatest factor shared by \(24\) and \(100\) is \(4\), so divide both the numerator and the denominator by \(4\). $$\frac{24}{100} = \frac{24 \div 4}{100 \div 4} = \frac{6}{25}$$ Since \(6\) and \(25\) share no common factor larger than \(1\), this is fully reduced. \(\boxed{6/25}\)

review.x: A loyalty card stamps your total at \(320\%\) of last month. Write \(320\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: The word percent means per hundred, so \(320\%\) is just \(320\) over \(100\). Write that fraction first, then simplify.
Both \(320\) and \(100\) share the factor \(20\). Divide top and bottom by \(20\) to land in lowest terms.
review.sol: Percent means per hundred, so \(320\%=\tfrac{320}{100}\). The greatest common factor of \(320\) and \(100\) is \(20\), so divide both parts by it. That gives \(\tfrac{320\div 20}{100\div 20}=\tfrac{16}{5}\). Since \(16\) and \(5\) share no factor bigger than \(1\), this is lowest terms, and it is more than one whole because \(16>5\). \(\boxed{16/5}\)

review.x: A deep freezer drifted \(-8\%\) from its set point. Write \(-8\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: A percent is a count out of one hundred, so \(-8\%\) means \(-8\) over \(100\). The negative sign rides along with the whole fraction.
Now reduce \(\tfrac{-8}{100}\). Both \(8\) and \(100\) share a factor of \(4\), so divide the top and the bottom by \(4\).
review.sol: Percent means per hundred, so the number sits over \(100\) and keeps its sign. That gives \(-8\% = \tfrac{-8}{100}\). The top and bottom share a common factor of \(4\), so divide both by \(4\). The numerator becomes \(-8 \div 4 = -2\) and the denominator becomes \(100 \div 4 = 25\). Since \(2\) and \(25\) have no common factor left, the fraction is in lowest terms. So \(-8\%\) equals \(\boxed{-2/25}\).

review.x: A weather app says the chance of rain is \(73\%\). Write \(73\%\) as a decimal.
hints: A percent means parts per hundred, so \(73\%\) is the same as \(\tfrac{73}{100}\).
Dividing by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the left, so \(73.\) becomes \(0.73\).
review.sol: A percent is just a number of hundredths, so \(73\%\) means \(\tfrac{73}{100}\). Dividing by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the left, turning \(73.\) into \(0.73\). So the decimal form is \(\boxed{0.73}\).

review.x: Write \(108\%\) as a decimal.
hints: A percent means hundredths, so \(108\%\) is \(108\) hundredths. To turn a percent into a decimal, divide by \(100\), which slides the point two places left.
Start with \(108.\) and walk the decimal point two steps to the left, past the \(8\) and past the \(0\).
review.sol: A percent is a number out of \(100\), so to write it as a decimal you divide by \(100\), which slides the point two places to the left. Begin with \(108\), really \(108.0\), and move the point two places left to land between the \(1\) and the first \(0\). That gives \(108\% = \tfrac{108}{100} = 1.08\). Notice the result is more than \(1\), which makes sense because \(108\%\) is more than a whole. So the decimal is \(\boxed{1.08}\).

review.x: A lab label reads \(0.6\%\) saline. Write \(0.6\%\) as a decimal.
hints: A percent means per hundred, so \(0.6\%\) is the same as \(\tfrac{0.6}{100}\). Dividing by 100 slides the decimal point two places to the left.
Start at \(0.6\) and move the point two places left. You will need to write in placeholder zeros to fill the empty spots.
review.sol: A percent is just a number over 100, so \(0.6\%\) means \(\tfrac{0.6}{100}\). Dividing by 100 slides the decimal point two places to the left. Starting from \(0.6\), one place left gives \(0.06\), and a second place gives \(0.006\), with placeholder zeros filling the gaps. So \(0.6\%\) as a decimal is \(\boxed{0.006}\).

review.x: A probe is filled to \(0.47\) of its tank. Write \(0.47\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: A decimal becomes a percent when you multiply by \(100\). Multiplying by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the right.
Start at \(0.47\) and move the point past the \(4\) and the \(7\). Where does it land, and what whole number is left?
review.sol: To turn a decimal into a percent, multiply by \(100\), which slides the point two places to the right. So \(0.47 \times 100 = 47\), giving \(47\%\). You can also picture it as \(0.47 = \tfrac{47}{100}\), and a denominator of \(100\) reads straight off as \(47\) per hundred. The number to post is \(\boxed{47}\).

review.x: A grain of gold dust registers \(0.025\) of a carat. Write \(0.025\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: Turning a decimal into a percent means multiplying by \(100\), which slides the decimal point two places to the right.
Start at \(0.025\) and move the point two places right. The first jump gives \(0.25\), the second gives \(2.5\), so the leftover digit keeps the percent a decimal.
review.sol: To go from a decimal to a percent, multiply by \(100\), which slides the decimal point two places to the right. $$0.025 \times 100 = 2.5$$ Sliding the point from \(0.025\) two places to the right lands between the \(2\) and the \(5\), so the value is \(2.5\%\). The number alone is \(\boxed{2.5}\).

review.x: A trail map marks \(\tfrac{7}{20}\) of its loops as beginner-friendly. Write \(\tfrac{7}{20}\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: A percent is a count out of \(100\), so turn the bottom number into \(100\). What do you multiply \(20\) by to land on \(100\)?
Since \(20 \times 5 = 100\), multiply the top by \(5\) too. Then the numerator over \(100\) is your percent number.
review.sol: A percent is just a fraction with a denominator of \(100\), so the plan is to rewrite \(\tfrac{7}{20}\) with \(100\) on the bottom. Multiplying \(20\) by \(5\) gives \(100\), so multiply the top by \(5\) as well to keep the same value. $$\frac{7}{20} = \frac{7 \times 5}{20 \times 5} = \frac{35}{100}$$ A numerator of \(35\) out of \(100\) means \(35\%\), so the percent number is \(\boxed{35}\).

review.x: A juice bottle is \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) real fruit. Write \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: A fraction becomes a percent when you multiply by \(100\). So you want \(\tfrac{5}{8}\times 100\).
Compute \(\tfrac{5\times 100}{8}=\tfrac{500}{8}\), then divide. The result is not a whole number, so let it land on a decimal.
review.sol: To turn a fraction into a percent, multiply it by \(100\). $$\frac{5}{8}\times 100=\frac{500}{8}=62.5$$ So \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) of the bottle is \(62.5\%\) fruit, and the number is \(\boxed{62.5}\).

review.x: Write \(\tfrac{11}{5}\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: To turn any fraction into a percent, multiply it by \(100\). The result is how many parts per hundred the fraction is worth.
Compute \(\tfrac{11}{5}\times 100\). One clean route is \(11\times 100=1{,}100\), then divide by \(5\).
review.sol: A percent is just a count of parts per hundred, so to write a fraction as a percent you multiply it by \(100\). That gives \(\tfrac{11}{5}\times 100=\tfrac{1{,}100}{5}\). Dividing, \(1{,}100\div 5=220\), so \(\tfrac{11}{5}=220\%\). That makes sense, since \(\tfrac{11}{5}\) is more than \(2\), and \(220\%\) is more than \(200\%\). The number is \(\boxed{220}\).

review.x: A pool is \(87.5\%\) full. Write \(87.5\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Percent means per hundred, so \(87.5\%\) is \(\tfrac{87.5}{100}\). Clear the decimal by multiplying the top and bottom by \(10\) to get \(\tfrac{875}{1000}\).
Both \(875\) and \(1{,}000\) share the factor \(125\). Divide each by \(125\) and see what is left.
review.sol: Percent means per hundred, so \(87.5\%=\tfrac{87.5}{100}\). Multiply the top and bottom by \(10\) to clear the decimal, which gives \(\tfrac{875}{1000}\). Now reduce. Both numbers are divisible by \(125\), and \(875\div125=7\) while \(1{,}000\div125=8\), so the fraction becomes \(\tfrac{7}{8}\). Since \(7\) is prime and does not divide \(8\), this is lowest terms. \(\boxed{7/8}\)
review.label: r13

review.x: Option 1 is \(\tfrac{9}{20}\) and option 2 is \(44\%\). Which value is larger? Type \(1\) or \(2\).
hints: To compare fairly, rewrite both as the same form. Turn the fraction into a percent so it sits next to \(44\%\).
Scale \(\tfrac{9}{20}\) up to a denominator of \(100\) by multiplying top and bottom by \(5\). What percent does that give you?
review.sol: Put both into percent form so they can be compared head to head. For the fraction, scale \(\tfrac{9}{20}\) to a denominator of \(100\) by multiplying top and bottom by \(5\), which gives \(\tfrac{45}{100}=45\%\). Now line them up, \(45\%\) against \(44\%\). Since \(45\%>44\%\), option 1 is the larger value. \(\boxed{1}\)

blocks.x: Part = percent × whole. The word "of" between a percent and a number means multiply. A jacket at \(25\%\) off, a tip at \(18\%\), a poll where \(60\%\) agree, each is just that one relationship read from a different corner.
A wildlife reserve counts \(200\) nesting birds, and \(25\%\) are herons. How many herons are there?
hints: The word "of" means multiply. Rewrite \(25\%\) as \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) or \(0.25\), then multiply it by \(200\).
One quarter of \(200\) is \(200 \div 4 = 50\). Or \(0.25 \times 200 = 50\).
blocks.sol: A percent of a number is a multiplication, with "of" as the times sign. First convert the percent, so \(25\% = \tfrac{1}{4} = 0.25\). Then multiply by the whole, $$25\% \text{ of } 200 = \tfrac{1}{4} \times 200 = 50.$$ The same answer comes from \(0.25 \times 200 = 50\). So there are \(\boxed{50}\) herons.

blocks.x: A bakery sold \(35\) loaves, and \(60\%\) were sourdough. How many sourdough loaves were sold?
hints: Rewrite \(60\%\) as \(0.6\) (or \(\tfrac{3}{5}\)), then multiply by \(35\).
blocks.sol: Convert and multiply. Since \(60\% = \tfrac{3}{5} = 0.6\), $$60\% \text{ of } 35 = \tfrac{3}{5} \times 35 = 3 \times 7 = 21.$$ So \(\boxed{21}\) sourdough loaves were sold.

blocks.x: A startup had \(15\) employees and grew to \(300\%\) of that size. How many employees are there now?
hints: A percent over \(100\) becomes a number bigger than \(1\). Here \(300\% = 3\).
Multiply \(3 \times 15 = 45\).
blocks.sol: Convert the percent, \(300\% = \tfrac{300}{100} = 3\). Then $$300\% \text{ of } 15 = 3 \times 15 = 45.$$ Notice the answer is larger than the original \(15\), which makes sense because a percent above \(100\) gives more than the whole. So there are \(\boxed{45}\) employees.

blocks.x: "Of" means multiply. To find a percent of a number, turn the percent into a fraction or a decimal and multiply, since the word "of" between a percent and a number always means times. This is just part \(=\) percent \(\times\) whole. The size of that multiplier is a built-in check, because taking \(100\%\) leaves a number unchanged, taking less than \(100\%\) makes it smaller, and taking more than \(100\%\) makes it larger.

blocks.cap: Finding \(25\%\) of \(200\). Split the whole bar of \(200\) into four equal quarters of \(50\). One quarter is \(25\%\), so \(25\%\) of \(200\) is one of those quarters, \(\tfrac{1}{4} \times 200 = 50\). Taking a percent of a number is multiplying the whole by that fraction.

blocks.x: A theater holds \(64\) seats, and \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\%\) are reserved for staff. Since \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\% = \tfrac{1}{8}\), how many seats are reserved?
hints: One eighth of \(64\) is \(64 \div 8 = 8\).
blocks.sol: Convert the fractional percent to a clean fraction, \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\% = \tfrac{12.5}{100} = \tfrac{1}{8}\). Then $$12\tfrac{1}{2}\% \text{ of } 64 = \tfrac{1}{8} \times 64 = 8.$$ So \(\boxed{8}\) seats are reserved.

blocks.x: A water sample weighs \(80\) g, and \(2.5\%\) is dissolved minerals. How many grams of minerals are there?
hints: Slide the point two places left, so \(2.5\% = 0.025\).
blocks.sol: Convert the percent to a decimal by sliding the point two places left, \(2.5\% = 0.025\). Then $$2.5\% \text{ of } 80 = 0.025 \times 80 = 2.$$ So there are \(\boxed{2}\) grams of dissolved minerals.

blocks.x: A class read \(30\) books out of a goal of \(120\). What percent of the goal did they reach? Give just the number.
hints: Make the fraction part over whole, \(\tfrac{30}{120}\), and simplify.
blocks.sol: To find what percent one number is of another, divide the part by the whole, then write it as a percent. $$\tfrac{30}{120} = \tfrac{1}{4} = 0.25 = 25\%.$$ So \(30\) is \(\boxed{25}\) percent of \(120\).

blocks.x: A phone battery holds \(80\) units full and \(60\) units now. What percent of \(80\) is \(60\)? Give just the number.
hints: The part is \(60\) and the whole is \(80\), so form \(\tfrac{60}{80}\) and simplify.
blocks.sol: Divide the part by the whole, then convert to a percent. $$\tfrac{60}{80} = \tfrac{3}{4} = 0.75 = 75\%.$$ So \(60\) is \(\boxed{75}\) percent of \(80\).

blocks.x: A shop bought a desk for \(\$17\) and resold it for \(\$51\). What percent is \(51\) of \(17\)? Give just the number.
hints: Form \(\tfrac{51}{17}\). Since \(51\) is bigger than \(17\), expect a percent above \(100\).
blocks.sol: Divide the part by the whole, \(\tfrac{51}{17} = 3\). As a percent that is \(3 = 300\%\). The resale price is three times the cost, so \(51\) is \(\boxed{300}\) percent of \(17\).

blocks.x: Percent \(=\) part \(\div\) whole. To find what percent one number is of another, divide the part by the whole and write the result as a percent. It is the same relationship read from a new corner, since a percent is just the part measured against the whole and then scaled to one hundred.

blocks.cap: The three percent questions are one relationship, \(\text{part} = \text{percent} \times \text{whole}\), read from three corners. Cover the part and you multiply percent by whole. Cover the percent and you divide the part by the whole. Cover the whole and you divide the part by the percent.

blocks.x: A scholarship covers \(\$80\) of a fee, and that is exactly \(20\%\) of the full fee. \(80\) is \(20\%\) of what number?
hints: The relationship is part equals percent times whole, so whole equals part divided by percent. Use \(20\% = 0.2\).
blocks.sol: Since the part is the percent times the whole, the whole is the part divided by the percent. With \(20\% = 0.2\), $$\text{whole} = \frac{80}{0.2} = 400.$$ Check it, \(20\%\) of \(400\) is \(0.2 \times 400 = 80\), which matches. So \(80\) is \(20\%\) of \(\boxed{400}\).

blocks.x: A hiker has walked \(27\) km, which is \(75\%\) of the full trail. \(27\) is \(75\%\) of what number?
hints: Whole equals part divided by percent, so compute \(27 \div 0.75\).
blocks.sol: Divide the part by the percent. With \(75\% = \tfrac{3}{4}\), $$\text{whole} = 27 \div \tfrac{3}{4} = 27 \times \tfrac{4}{3} = 36.$$ Check, \(\tfrac{3}{4} \times 36 = 27\), which matches. The trail is \(\boxed{36}\) kilometers long.

blocks.x: Whole \(=\) part \(\div\) percent. When you know the part and the percent but not the whole, divide the part by the percent, written as a fraction or a decimal, to recover the whole. This undoes the multiplication, so always check by taking that percent of your answer to see if it lands back on the part.
Ancient Rome funded its army with a \(1\%\) levy on public auctions. Counting in hundredths made tax rates easy to compare and collect, the same reason a percent is handy today.
A startup's revenue this month is \(\$18\)k, which is \(150\%\) of last month's. \(18\) is \(150\%\) of what number?
hints: Whole equals part divided by percent, so compute \(18 \div 1.5\).
\(18 \div 1.5 = 12\). Since the percent is over \(100\), the whole is smaller than the part.
blocks.sol: Divide the part by the percent, with \(150\% = 1.5\). $$\text{whole} = \frac{18}{1.5} = 12.$$ Because \(150\%\) is more than \(100\%\), the answer \(12\) is smaller than the part \(18\), which fits. Check, \(1.5 \times 12 = 18\). So last month's revenue was \(\boxed{12}\) thousand dollars.

blocks.x: A museum finds \(30\%\) of \(80\) visitors came by bus. What percent of \(120\) school visitors is that same bus count? Give just the number.
hints: Step one, find \(30\%\) of \(80\). That is \(0.3 \times 80 = 24\).
Step two, what percent is \(24\) of \(120\)? Form \(\tfrac{24}{120} = 0.2 = 20\%\).
blocks.sol: Do it in two steps. First the part, \(30\%\) of \(80 = 0.3 \times 80 = 24\). Then ask what percent \(24\) is of \(120\), $$\tfrac{24}{120} = \tfrac{1}{5} = 0.2 = 20\%.$$ So \(30\%\) of \(80\) is \(\boxed{20}\) percent of \(120\).

review.x: A farmers market had \(90\) stalls, and \(40\%\) sold vegetables. How many stalls sold vegetables?
hints: "Of" means multiply. Rewrite \(40\% = 0.4\), then multiply by \(90\).
review.sol: \(40\% = 0.4\), so \(40\% \text{ of } 90 = 0.4 \times 90 = 36\). So \(\boxed{36}\) stalls sold vegetables.

review.x: A song had \(12\) thousand listeners last week and climbed to \(250\%\) of that this week. How many thousand listeners this week?
hints: A percent over \(100\) is more than \(1\). Here \(250\% = 2.5\).
review.sol: \(250\% = \tfrac{250}{100} = 2.5\), so \(250\% \text{ of } 12 = 2.5 \times 12 = 30\). So there are \(\boxed{30}\) thousand listeners.

review.x: A puzzle has \(16\) pieces, and \(37\tfrac{1}{2}\%\) are edge pieces. How many edge pieces are there?
hints: Convert the fractional percent, \(37\tfrac{1}{2}\% = \tfrac{37.5}{100} = \tfrac{3}{8}\).
review.sol: \(37\tfrac{1}{2}\% = \tfrac{37.5}{100} = \tfrac{3}{8}\), so \(\tfrac{3}{8} \times 16 = 6\). So there are \(\boxed{6}\) edge pieces.

review.x: A reservoir holds \(150\) million liters, and \(8\%\) is in reserve. How many million liters are in reserve?
hints: Rewrite \(8\% = 0.08\), then multiply by \(150\).
review.sol: \(8\% = 0.08\), so \(8\% \text{ of } 150 = 0.08 \times 150 = 12\). So \(\boxed{12}\) million liters are in reserve.

review.x: A team won \(18\) of its \(72\) games. What percent did the team win? Give just the number.
hints: Form part over whole, \(\tfrac{18}{72}\), and simplify.
review.sol: \(\tfrac{18}{72} = \tfrac{1}{4} = 0.25 = 25\%\). So the team won \(\boxed{25}\) percent of its games.

review.x: A jar holds \(40\) marbles and \(30\) are blue. What percent of \(40\) is \(30\)? Give just the number.
hints: Form \(\tfrac{30}{40}\) and simplify.

review.x: A sequel sold \(84\) thousand copies; the original sold \(56\) thousand. What percent is \(84\) of \(56\)? Give just the number.
hints: Form \(\tfrac{84}{56}\). Since \(84\) is bigger than \(56\), expect over \(100\).

review.x: A deposit of \(\$21\) is \(30\%\) of a bike's total price. \(21\) is \(30\%\) of what number?
hints: Whole equals part divided by percent, so compute \(21 \div 0.3\).
review.sol: Whole equals part divided by percent, with \(30\% = 0.3\), so \(\tfrac{21}{0.3} = 70\). Check, \(0.3 \times 70 = 21\). So the price is \(\boxed{70}\) dollars.

review.x: A runner has finished \(45\) laps, which is \(90\%\) of the race. \(45\) is \(90\%\) of what number?
hints: Compute \(45 \div 0.9\).
review.sol: Whole equals part divided by percent, with \(90\% = 0.9\), so \(\tfrac{45}{0.9} = 50\). Check, \(0.9 \times 50 = 45\). So the race is \(\boxed{50}\) laps.

review.x: This year a town recorded \(120\) building permits, which is \(150\%\) of last year's. \(120\) is \(150\%\) of what number?
hints: Compute \(120 \div 1.5\). Since the percent is over \(100\), the whole is smaller than \(120\).
review.sol: Whole equals part divided by percent, with \(150\% = 1.5\), so \(\tfrac{120}{1.5} = 80\). Check, \(1.5 \times 80 = 120\). So last year had \(\boxed{80}\) permits.

review.x: A tasting portion is \(5\) g, which is \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\%\) of a full serving. \(5\) is \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\%\) of what number?
hints: Convert \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\% = \tfrac{1}{8}\), then whole equals part divided by percent.
review.sol: Convert the percent, \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\% = \tfrac{1}{8}\). Whole equals part divided by percent, so \(5 \div \tfrac{1}{8} = 5 \times 8 = 40\). Check, \(\tfrac{1}{8} \times 40 = 5\). So a full serving is \(\boxed{40}\) grams.

review.x: Chain two steps: take \(60\%\) of \(500\), then take \(20\%\) of that result. What is \(20\%\) of \(60\%\) of \(500\)?

blocks.x: In 9.2 you took a percent of a fixed number. Now the number moves. A \(30\%\) rise means the new amount is \(130\%\) of the original, one multiply by \(1.30\), the multiplier. A drop of \(20\%\) leaves \(80\%\), a single multiply by \(0.80\).
A garden trail of \(240\) m was extended by \(35\%\). Find \(35\%\) of \(240\), then add it to \(240\). How many meters of trail are there now?
hints: The extra trail is \(35\%\) of the ORIGINAL \(240\) meters, not of some new total. Use part \(=\) percent \(\times\) whole, so the added length is \(0.35 \times 240\).
Once you know the added piece is \(84\) meters, the new trail is just the old length plus that piece, \(240 + 84\). Adding the extra back on is what makes this an increase.
blocks.sol: Walk it the slow way first. The increase is \(35\%\) of the original \(240\) meters, so using part \(=\) percent \(\times\) whole gives $$0.35 \times 240 = 84 \text{ meters added.}$$ Now add that piece back onto where you started, \(240 + 84 = 324\). There is a faster route that lands in the same place. Keeping all of the original is \(100\%\) of it, and adding \(35\%\) more makes \(135\%\) in all, so the multiplier is \(1 + 0.35 = 1.35\). That gives \(240 \times 1.35 = 324\) in one step. Either way the trail is now \(\boxed{324}\) meters long.

blocks.x: A streaming channel had \(480\) subscribers and grew by \(35\%\). The new count is \(135\%\) of \(480\). How many subscribers does the channel have now?
hints: The original count is \(100\%\) of itself, and an increase piles more on top of that. A \(35\%\) increase means the new amount is \(100\% + 35\% = 135\%\) of the original.
Turn \(135\%\) into the multiplier \(1.35\), then compute \(480 \times 1.35\) in one step instead of adding the part separately.
blocks.sol: A \(35\%\) increase keeps the whole original and adds \(35\%\) more, so the new count is \(100\% + 35\% = 135\%\) of \(480\). Written as a multiplier that is \(1 + \tfrac{35}{100} = 1.35\). Multiply all at once. $$480 \times 1.35 = 648$$ Check it against the add-on path. \(35\%\) of \(480\) is \(0.35 \times 480 = 168\), and \(480 + 168 = 648\), the same result. The channel now has \(\boxed{648}\) subscribers.

blocks.x: Increase multiplier. A \(p\%\) rise takes \(p\%\) of the amount you started with and adds it back onto that same start, so the new total is the original plus a slice of itself. Since the original is already the full \(100\%\), growing by \(p\%\) lands you at \((100+p)\%\) of the start, which you can do in one step by multiplying by \(1 + \frac{p}{100}\). A \(30\%\) rise is a single multiply by \(1.30\).
A coastal marsh had \(350\) wading birds at dawn. By midday, \(12\%\) had flown off. The survivors are \(88\%\) of \(350\). How many birds remain at midday?
hints: A \(12\%\) decrease does not leave \(12\%\); it leaves the rest. Find the leftover percent by taking \(100\% - 12\% = 88\%\), and that fraction of the flock is what you want.

Turn \(88\%\) into the multiplier \(0.88\) and multiply the original count by it, \(350 \times 0.88\). If you prefer the long way, take \(12\%\) of \(350\) and subtract it from \(350\); both roads land on the same number.
blocks.sol: A drop of \(12\%\) takes away \(12\%\) and leaves the other \(88\%\), since \(100\% - 12\% = 88\%\). So the multiplier for a \(12\%\) decrease is \(1 - 0.12 = 0.88\), and the midday count is $$350 \times 0.88 = 308.$$ The slower route agrees. First \(12\%\) of \(350\) is \(0.12 \times 350 = 42\) birds that flew off, and the survivors are \(350 - 42 = 308\). Both paths give the same flock, so \(308\) wading birds are still present at midday. \(\boxed{308}\)

blocks.x: A kayak rental costs \(\$90\) a day. A \(40\%\) discount removes \(40\%\) and leaves \(60\%\). What is the discounted price in dollars?
hints: A \(40\%\) discount removes \(40\%\) of the price, so what is left is \(100\% - 40\% = 60\%\) of it. That surviving \(60\%\) is your multiplier.
The multiplier for a \(40\%\) decrease is \(1 - 0.40 = 0.60\). Multiply the original price by it, \(90 \times 0.60\).
blocks.sol: A \(40\%\) decrease multiplies by \(1 - \tfrac{40}{100} = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60\), the share of the price that survives the discount. So the new price is $$90 \times 0.60 = 54.$$ You can check it the slow way. Forty percent of \(90\) is \(0.40 \times 90 = 36\), and subtracting gives \(90 - 36 = 54\), the same answer. The multiplier just folds the subtracting into one step. \(\boxed{54}\)

blocks.x: Decrease multiplier. When you cut \(p\%\) off, what is left is the rest of the hundred, so removing \(12\%\) leaves \(88\%\) standing, a single multiply by \(0.88\). A \(p\%\) decrease multiplies by \(1 - \frac{p}{100}\), which is why a \(20\%\) discount is just \(80\%\) of the price. Increase lifts the multiplier above \(1\) and decrease pulls it below \(1\), the same one-multiply move both ways.

blocks.cap: Both strips start from the same original, the full \(100\%\) bar. Adding \(30\%\) stacks a new block on the right, so the total is \(100\% + 30\% = 130\%\), which is the multiplier \(\times 1.30\). Cutting \(20\%\) peels a slice off the right, leaving \(100\% - 20\% = 80\%\), the multiplier \(\times 0.80\). An increase always lands above \(1\) and a decrease always lands below \(1\), and either way you are scaling the original \(100\%\).

blocks.x: A podcast went from \(250\) plays to \(320\). The change is \(70\) plays. Divide by the original \(250\), not the new \(320\). What percent increase was this? Give the number only.
hints: First find the change in plays, the new amount minus the old amount. Then remember a percent change is measured against the ORIGINAL, so the comparison you want is that change divided by \(250\), not divided by \(320\).
You have \(70\) more plays out of the starting \(250\). Compute \(\tfrac{70}{250}\) and turn that fraction into a percent by multiplying by \(100\).
blocks.sol: First find how much the plays grew, \(320 - 250 = 70\) more plays. A percent change is that change measured against the original amount, so divide by the \(250\) you started with, not the \(320\) you ended with. That gives the fraction \(\tfrac{70}{250}\). To read it as a percent, multiply by \(100\), so $$\frac{70}{250} \times 100 = 28.$$ You can check it with the multiplier idea. A \(28\%\) increase scales the original by \(1.28\), and \(250 \times 1.28 = 320\), which lands exactly on the second episode. The question names the direction, so the answer is the positive size of the increase, \(\boxed{28}\).

blocks.x: A library had \(640\) visitors on a sunny Saturday and \(480\) on a rainy one, a drop of \(160\). Divide by the original \(640\). What percent decrease was this? Give the number only.
hints: First find the drop, then ask what fraction of the ORIGINAL it is. The drop is \(640 - 480 = 160\), and the original is \(640\), so the percent change is \(\tfrac{160}{640}\) written as a percent.

Simplify \(\tfrac{160}{640}\). Both top and bottom share a factor of \(160\), so it becomes \(\tfrac{1}{4}\), and \(\tfrac{1}{4} = 25\%\). Resist the urge to divide by \(480\); the original count is the whole here.
blocks.sol:
The drop in visitors is \(640 - 480 = 160\). A percent change always measures that change against the original amount, so divide by \(640\), not by \(480\).

$$\frac{640 - 480}{640} = \frac{160}{640} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

You can check it with the multiplier. A drop of \(25\%\) means the rainy Saturday kept \(1 - 0.25 = 0.75\) of the sunny crowd, and \(0.75 \times 640 = 480\), which matches. If you had divided by \(480\) instead you would get about \(33\%\), but that answers a different question, how much you would need to grow to climb back to \(640\). For the decrease from the original, the answer is \(\boxed{25}\).

blocks.x: A bamboo shoot grew from \(14\) cm to \(35\) cm, a change of \(21\) cm. Since \(21 > 14\), the percent will sail past \(100\%\). What percent increase was this? Give the number only.
hints: The change is \(35 - 14 = 21\) centimeters. For a percent change you always divide by the ORIGINAL amount, the \(14\) centimeters it started at, not the \(35\) it ended at.
Compute \(\tfrac{21}{14}\) and turn it into a percent. Since \(21\) is more than \(14\), this fraction is bigger than \(1\), so the percent lands above \(100\%\).
blocks.sol: First find the change, \(35 - 14 = 21\) centimeters. A percent change is measured against the original, so divide by \(14\), not by \(35\). $$\frac{35 - 14}{14} = \frac{21}{14} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$$ As a percent that is \(150\%\). You can sanity check it with the multiplier. Going from \(14\) to \(35\) means multiplying by \(\tfrac{35}{14} = 2.5\), and a multiplier of \(2.5\) is the same as \(1 + 1.5\), a \(150\%\) increase. That fits the warning in the problem. A plain doubling to \(28\) would have been a \(100\%\) increase, and this rose past double, so the percent climbs above \(100\%\). \(\boxed{150}\)

blocks.x: Percent change \(=\) change \(\div\) original. Find how much it changed, the new minus the old, then divide by the original amount and write the result as a percent. It is an increase when the number grew and a decrease when it shrank. The original is always the yardstick in the denominator, since the question is how big the change was compared to where you started. Doubling adds one whole extra copy, so it is a \(100\%\) increase, not \(200\%\); a rise has no ceiling, but a decrease is capped at \(100\%\), since once everything is gone there is nothing left to remove.

blocks.cap: A podcast climbs from \(250\) plays to \(320\). The gold slice is the change, \(320 - 250 = 70\), and the percent change is that change measured against where we started, \(\tfrac{70}{250} = 0.28 = 28\%\). Dividing by the new amount, \(\tfrac{70}{320}\), is the trap, since the change is always compared to the original, never to where you landed.

blocks.x: "Percentage points" and "percent change" differ. A rate rising from \(5\%\) to \(7\%\) is \(2\) percentage points (subtraction) but a \(40\%\) increase (\(2\div5\)). Headlines often swap them.
After a \(20\%\) increase, a shelf holds 738 books. To recover last year's count, divide by \(1.20\), not subtract \(20\%\) of 738. How many books were there before?
hints: The tempting wrong path is \(738 - 20\%\) of \(738 = 590.4\), which is not even a whole number of books. The \(20\%\) growth was a slice of last year's count, not of this year's. So the right question is what number, when grown by \(20\%\), lands on \(738\).
A \(20\%\) increase multiplies the original by \(1.20\), so \(1.20 \times \text{original} = 738\). To undo a multiplication, divide. Compute \(738 \div 1.20\).
blocks.sol: First see why subtracting fails. The wrong path gives \(738 - 0.20 \times 738 = 738 - 147.6 = 590.4\), and a shelf cannot hold \(590.4\) books. The reason is that the \(20\%\) was a slice of LAST year's count, not of this year's \(738\), so peeling \(20\%\) off the new total takes off too much. Set it up with the multiplier instead. A \(20\%\) increase means the new amount is \(1.20\) times the original, so $$1.20 \times \text{original} = 738.$$ Undo the multiplication by dividing both sides by \(1.20\), which gives \(\text{original} = 738 \div 1.20 = 615\). Check it by growing \(615\) by \(20\%\), since \(1.20 \times 615 = 615 + 123 = 738\), exactly the new count. Last year the shelf held \(\boxed{615}\) books.

blocks.x: A ferry pass now costs \(\$510\) after a \(15\%\) fare cut. A \(15\%\) decrease multiplied the old price by \(0.85\). What was the original season pass price in dollars?
hints: A \(15\%\) decrease keeps \(100\% - 15\% = 85\%\) of the original, so it multiplies the old price by \(0.85\) to give \(510\). The \(510\) is the new amount, not the original.

Since \(\text{original} \times 0.85 = 510\), undo the multiplier by dividing. Compute \(510 \div 0.85\). Adding \(15\%\) of \(510\) back on is the wrong move, because that \(15\%\) would be a slice of the smaller current price, not of the original.
blocks.sol: A \(15\%\) decrease leaves \(100\% - 15\% = 85\%\) of the original price, so the multiplier is \(0.85\). That multiplier acted on the old price to produce the current one, which means \(\text{original} \times 0.85 = 510\). To recover the original, divide the new amount by the multiplier. $$\text{original} = \frac{510}{0.85} = 600$$ Check it forward, \(600 \times 0.85 = 510\), which matches. Notice why the tempting shortcut fails. Adding \(15\%\) of \(510\) back gives \(510 + 76.50 = 586.50\), an overshoot, because \(15\%\) of the smaller current price is smaller than \(15\%\) of the original. The original season pass price was \(\boxed{600}\) dollars.

blocks.x: A bee colony of \(200\) grew by \(30\%\) one week, then shrank by \(30\%\) the next. Many guess it returns to \(200\). It does not. How many workers are in the colony at the end?
hints: Do the two weeks in order, one change at a time. A \(30\%\) increase multiplies by \(1.30\), so the boosted colony is \(200 \times 1.30\). Whatever that comes to is the new starting size for week two.

For week two, a \(30\%\) decrease multiplies by \(0.70\). The catch is that the \(30\%\) is taken off the bigger boosted number, not off the original \(200\), so it removes more bees than the increase added.
blocks.sol: Handle the weeks in order, each as a multiplier. A \(30\%\) increase multiplies by \(1.30\), and a \(30\%\) decrease multiplies by \(0.70\). After week one, \(200 \times 1.30 = 260\) workers. After week two, the cold snap takes \(30\%\) off that boosted \(260\), so \(260 \times 0.70 = 182\). Notice the two multipliers chain together as \(1.30 \times 0.70 = 0.91\), which is less than \(1\), so the colony lands below where it began. The \(30\%\) drop was taken from the larger number \(260\), so it stripped away more bees than the earlier \(30\%\) boost had added. The colony ends at \(\boxed{182}\) workers.

blocks.x: Undo by dividing, chain by multiplying. The new amount is the original times the multiplier, so original \(=\) new \(\div\) multiplier; after a \(20\%\) rise, divide by \(1.20\) to climb back. Do not subtract the same percent off the new amount, since that percent was a slice of the smaller original and would peel off too much. Sequential changes multiply, they do not add, because each change acts on a different amount. That is why a \(p\%\) rise then a \(p\%\) fall never returns you to the start, since \(\left(1+\frac{p}{100}\right)\!\left(1-\frac{p}{100}\right)\) is always a hair under \(1\).

blocks.cap: Going up \(20\%\) then down \(20\%\) does not undo itself. The rise multiplies by \(1.20\) and the cut multiplies by \(0.80\), and \(1.20 \times 0.80 = 0.96\), so you land at \(100 \times 0.96 = 96\), a hair below the dashed start line, not back at \(100\). The reason is that the \(20\%\) cut came off the taller \(120\), which is \(24\), while the \(20\%\) rise had added only \(20\). Same percent, different size to act on, so the trip down outweighs the trip up.

blocks.x: Every move in this lesson is one multiplier: above \(1\) for an increase, below \(1\) for a decrease. A chain of changes is just those multipliers multiplied together in order, no guessing whether they cancel.

A recycling program collects \(5{,}000\) kg in Q1 and expects each later quarter to grow by \(10\%\). Each quarter multiplies by \(1.10\). How many kilograms are expected in Q4 (three quarters later)?
hints: A \(10\%\) increase multiplies the amount by \(1.10\). One quarter later it is \(5{,}000 \times 1.10\). Each new quarter multiplies the previous quarter's amount by \(1.10\) again.

Three quarters later means three multipliers stacked, so compute \(5{,}000 \times 1.10 \times 1.10 \times 1.10\). Take it one step at a time, \(5{,}000\) to \(5{,}500\) to \(6{,}050\), then once more.
blocks.sol: A \(10\%\) increase means multiplying by \(1 + \tfrac{10}{100} = 1.10\). Since the same percent applies each quarter, I stack the same multiplier once for each step. Stepping through it, the second quarter is \(5{,}000 \times 1.10 = 5{,}500\), the third quarter is \(5{,}500 \times 1.10 = 6{,}050\), and the fourth quarter is \(6{,}050 \times 1.10 = 6{,}655\). Notice the jumps grow, \(500\) then \(550\) then \(605\), because each \(10\%\) is taken from a bigger amount than before. All three steps at once are $$5{,}000 \times 1.10^3 = 5{,}000 \times 1.331 = 6{,}655.$$ So three quarters later the program expects \(\boxed{6655}\) kilograms.

blocks.x: An art class had \(400\) students. Enrollment rose \(50\%\) for summer, then fell \(40\%\) from that summer peak. A \(50\%\) rise and a \(40\%\) fall do not cancel. What is the fall enrollment?
hints: Do it in two steps, one change at a time. A \(50\%\) increase multiplies by \(1.50\), so the summer peak is \(400 \times 1.50\). Whatever that comes to, the fall drop of \(40\%\) is measured off that peak, not off the original \(400\).

A \(40\%\) decrease multiplies by \(1 - 0.40 = 0.60\), so multiply the summer peak by \(0.60\). If you like, chain the multipliers first, \(1.50 \times 0.60 = 0.90\), which already tells you the fall figure lands at \(90\%\) of the original, not back at \(100\%\).
blocks.sol: Take the two changes in order, each as a multiplier. The \(50\%\) increase multiplies by \(1 + 0.50 = 1.50\), so the summer peak is $$400 \times 1.50 = 600.$$ The fall drop of \(40\%\) is taken off that peak, and a \(40\%\) decrease multiplies by \(1 - 0.40 = 0.60\), so $$600 \times 0.60 = 360.$$ Here is the punchline. The two changes do not cancel. Chaining the multipliers gives \(1.50 \times 0.60 = 0.90\), which is a net \(90\%\) of the starting \(400\), a \(10\%\) drop overall. The reason is that the \(50\%\) rise was figured on \(400\) but the \(40\%\) fall was figured on the larger \(600\), so they sit on different bases and never quite undo each other. The fall enrollment is \(\boxed{360}\).

review.x: A bakery sold \(80\) loaves on Tuesday and \(15\%\) more on Wednesday. How many loaves did the bakery sell on Wednesday?
hints: A \(15\%\) increase means the new amount is the old amount plus \(15\%\) of it. As a multiplier that is \(1 + 0.15\).
Multiply Tuesday's \(80\) loaves by the multiplier \(1.15\) to get Wednesday's total.
review.sol: A \(15\%\) increase multiplies the original by \(1 + \tfrac{15}{100} = 1.15\), since the new amount is \(100\%\) of Tuesday plus another \(15\%\). So Wednesday's loaves are $$80 \times 1.15 = 92.$$ You can check this the long way. \(15\%\) of \(80\) is \(0.15 \times 80 = 12\), and \(80 + 12 = 92\), which matches. The bakery sold \(\boxed{92}\) loaves on Wednesday.

review.x: A winter jacket priced at \(\$250\) is marked \(30\%\) off. What is the sale price in dollars?
hints: Taking \(30\%\) off leaves \(100\% - 30\% = 70\%\) of the original price, so the multiplier is \(0.70\).
Multiply the old price by the decrease multiplier, \(250 \times 0.70\).
review.sol: A \(30\%\) decrease keeps \(70\%\) of the price, so the multiplier is \(1 - 0.30 = 0.70\). Apply it to the original price. $$250 \times 0.70 = 175$$ The sale price is \(\boxed{175}\) dollars.

review.x: A stadium that held \(600\) fans expanded its seating by \(45\%\). How many seats are there now?
hints: A \(45\%\) increase keeps the original \(100\%\) and adds \(45\%\) more, so the new amount is \(100\% + 45\% = 145\%\) of the old. As a multiplier that is \(1 + 0.45 = 1.45\).
Multiply the old capacity by that factor. Work out \(600 \times 1.45\).
review.sol: A \(45\%\) increase means the new seating is the whole \(100\%\) plus another \(45\%\), which is \(145\%\) of the old amount. Written as a multiplier that is \(1 + 0.45 = 1.45\). Now multiply the original capacity by it. $$600 \times 1.45 = 870$$ So the stadium now holds \(\boxed{870}\) seats.

review.x: A bamboo shoot rose from \(40\) cm to \(54\) cm, a change of \(14\) cm. Divide by the original. What percent increase is this? Give the number only.
hints: A percent change always compares the change to where you started, so divide the \(14\) by the original \(40\), not by \(54\).
Work out \(\tfrac{14}{40}\) as a decimal, then multiply by \(100\) to turn it into a percent.
review.sol: The shoot climbed from \(40\) up to \(54\), so the change is \(54 - 40 = 14\) centimeters. For a percent change you compare that change to the ORIGINAL height, so divide by \(40\). That gives \(\tfrac{14}{40} = 0.35\), and as a percent \(0.35 \times 100 = 35\%\). You can check it with the multiplier. A \(35\%\) increase multiplies by \(1 + \tfrac{35}{100} = 1.35\), and \(40 \times 1.35 = 54\), which matches the evening height. So the increase is \(\boxed{35}\).

review.x: A reservoir dropped from \(900\) to \(720\) megalitres, a fall of \(180\). What percent decrease is this? Give the number only.
hints: A percent change always compares the change to the ORIGINAL amount, not the amount you ended with. The drop is \(180\), and the original is \(900\), so look at \(\tfrac{180}{900}\).
Think of the multiplier. The new level is \(\tfrac{720}{900}\) of the original, which is \(0.80\), so the reservoir kept \(80\%\) and lost the rest.
review.sol:
The change is the drop, \(900 - 720 = 180\) megaliters. For a percent change you divide the change by the ORIGINAL amount, which is \(900\), not by the new \(720\).

$$\frac{180}{900} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20$$

So the level fell by \(20\%\). You can check this with the multiplier. The new amount over the original is \(\tfrac{720}{900} = 0.80\), meaning the reservoir holds \(80\%\) of what it did before, a \(20\%\) decrease.

\(\boxed{20}\)

review.x: A startup grew from \(25\) customers to \(75\), a jump of \(50\). Since \(50 > 25\), the percent lands above \(100\%\). What percent increase is this? Give the number only.
hints: Percent change always compares the change to the ORIGINAL amount, so divide by \(25\), not by \(75\). First find the change, which is \(75 - 25\).
The change is \(50\). Now compute \(\tfrac{50}{25}\) and turn that into a percent by multiplying by \(100\). Notice the new amount is three times the old, a multiplier of \(3\).
review.sol: First find the change in customers, which is \(75 - 25 = 50\). For a percent increase you compare this change to the ORIGINAL amount, so divide by \(25\), not by \(75\). That gives \(\tfrac{50}{25} = 2\), and \(2\) written as a percent is \(2 \times 100 = 200\%\). You can check this with the multiplier idea. The new count is \(75\), which is \(\tfrac{75}{25} = 3\) times the old count, a multiplier of \(3\). A multiplier of \(3\) means \(1 + 2\), and that extra \(2\) is the \(200\%\) increase sitting on top of the original \(100\%\). So the percent increase is \(\boxed{200}\).

review.x: After a \(25\%\) increase, a road bike sells for \(\$500\). The \(25\%\) was on the OLD price. What was the original price in dollars?
hints: A \(25\%\) increase multiplies the original by \(1 + \tfrac{25}{100} = 1.25\). So \(1.25\) times the old price equals the new price of \(500\).
To undo the multiplier, divide the new price by it. Work out \(500 \div 1.25\), and notice this is not the same as subtracting \(25\%\) of \(500\).
review.sol: A \(25\%\) increase scales the original price by the multiplier \(1 + \tfrac{25}{100} = 1.25\). That means \(1.25 \times \text{original} = 500\). To find the original, divide the new price by the multiplier. $$\text{original} = \frac{500}{1.25} = 400$$ Quick check, \(1.25 \times 400 = 500\), which matches. Notice that subtracting \(25\%\) of \(500\) would give \(375\), the wrong answer, because the \(25\%\) was taken on the smaller original, not on \(500\). The original price was \(\boxed{400}\) dollars.

review.x: After a \(20\%\) decrease, a sofa sells for \(\$480\). The multiplier was \(0.80\). What was the original price in dollars?
hints: A \(20\%\) decrease multiplies the original by \(1 - 0.20 = 0.80\), so the sale price is \(0.80\) times the original. You know the sale price, and you want the original.
To undo the multiplier, divide the new price by it. Compute \(480 \div 0.80\) to find the original.
review.sol: A \(20\%\) decrease means the sofa keeps \(100\% - 20\% = 80\%\) of its price, so the multiplier is \(1 - 0.20 = 0.80\). That gives the relationship $$\text{original} \times 0.80 = 480.$$ To find the original, divide the new price by the multiplier rather than adding back \(20\%\). $$\text{original} = \frac{480}{0.80} = 600.$$ As a check, a \(20\%\) cut from \(600\) is \(600 \times 0.80 = 480\), which matches. The original price was \(\boxed{600}\) dollars.

review.x: A vlogger started with \(200\) subscribers, gained \(40\%\), then lost \(25\%\) of that new total. How many subscribers does the channel have after both months?
hints: A \(40\%\) increase multiplies by \(1.40\), and a \(25\%\) decrease multiplies by \(0.75\). Each change works on whatever the count is right then, so apply them one after the other.
First find the count after month one by taking \(200 \times 1.40\). Then multiply that result by \(0.75\) for month two. Do not just cancel the \(40\%\) against the \(25\%\).
review.sol: A \(40\%\) increase scales by \(1 + 0.40 = 1.40\), and a \(25\%\) decrease scales by \(1 - 0.25 = 0.75\). Apply them in order to the starting count of \(200\). After the first month the channel has \(200 \times 1.40 = 280\) subscribers. After the second month it has \(280 \times 0.75 = 210\) subscribers. Notice that gaining \(40\%\) then losing \(25\%\) did not land back at \(200\), because the \(25\%\) drop came off the larger number \(280\). The final count is \(\boxed{210}\).

review.x: A koi pond holds \(2{,}000\) liters and gains \(20\%\) each day. How many liters does the pond hold after three full days of growth?
hints: A 20 percent increase means the new volume is 120 percent of the old one, so each day multiplies by \(1.20\). Three days in a row means doing that multiply three times.
Compute \(1.20^3\) first, then multiply by the starting \(2{,}000\) liters. You can also step through it day by day if that feels clearer.
review.sol: A \(20\%\) increase multiplies by \(1 + \tfrac{20}{100} = 1.20\), and three days of growth means applying that multiplier three times. So the final volume is $$2{,}000 \times 1.20 \times 1.20 \times 1.20 = 2{,}000 \times 1.20^3.$$ Stepping through it, day one gives \(2{,}000 \times 1.20 = 2{,}400\), day two gives \(2{,}400 \times 1.20 = 2{,}880\), and day three gives \(2{,}880 \times 1.20 = 3{,}456\). The pond holds \(\boxed{3456}\) liters after three days.

review.x: A factory used \(1{,}500\) kWh last month and cut usage by \(8\%\) this month. How many kWh did the factory use this month?
hints: A decrease keeps the rest of the original. If you let go of \(8\%\), how much do you hold onto?
Keeping \(92\%\) means multiplying the original by the multiplier \(0.92\).
review.sol: An \(8\%\) decrease leaves \(100\% - 8\% = 92\%\) of the original, so the multiplier is \(0.92\). Multiply the original amount by it. $$1{,}500 \times 0.92 = 1{,}380$$ So this month the factory used \(\boxed{1380}\) kilowatt-hours.

review.x: A scout troop grew from \(120\) to \(156\) members, a change of \(36\). Divide by the original. What percent increase is this? Give the number only.
hints: Percent change always divides the change by the amount you started with, so use \(\tfrac{36}{120}\), not \(\tfrac{36}{156}\).
Work out \(36 \div 120 = 0.30\), then turn that decimal into a percent by reading off the hundredths.
review.sol: The change is \(156 - 120 = 36\) members. For a percent change you divide the change by the original amount, so $$\frac{36}{120} = 0.30.$$ A decimal of \(0.30\) is \(30\%\), which means the troop multiplied by \(1.30\). You can check it, since \(120 \times 1.30 = 156\). So this is a \(30\%\) increase, and the answer is \(\boxed{30}\).

review.x: A camping tent is marked down \(40\%\) and now sells for \(\$90\). The sale price is \(60\%\) of the original. What was the original price in dollars?
hints: A \(40\%\) decrease multiplies the original by \(1 - 0.40\), so the sale price is \(0.60\) times the original.
To undo the multiplier, divide the new price by it. Work out \(90 \div 0.60\).
review.sol: A \(40\%\) decrease uses the multiplier \(1 - 0.40 = 0.60\), so the sale price equals \(0.60\) times the original price. That gives \(0.60 \times \text{original} = 90\). To find the original, divide the new price by the multiplier, not by subtracting the percent back. So \(\text{original} = \tfrac{90}{0.60} = 150\). Check it by taking \(40\%\) off, \(150 - 0.40 \times 150 = 150 - 60 = 90\), which matches the sale price. The original price was \(\boxed{150}\) dollars.

review.x: A club had \(\$500\). A fundraiser raised it by \(20\%\), then supply costs cut it by \(20\%\). How many dollars are left in the fund?
hints: A \(20\%\) increase multiplies by \(1.20\), and a \(20\%\) decrease multiplies by \(0.80\). Apply them one after the other to the \(500\).
First find \(500 \times 1.20\) to get the amount after the fundraiser, then multiply that result by \(0.80\) for the drop from supply costs.
review.sol: The fundraiser is a \(20\%\) increase, so it multiplies by \(1 + 0.20 = 1.20\). The supply costs are a \(20\%\) decrease, so they multiply by \(1 - 0.20 = 0.80\). Stack the two changes by multiplying the multipliers. $$500 \times 1.20 \times 0.80 = 600 \times 0.80 = 480$$ Notice the fund lands below \(500\), not back at it, because the \(20\%\) drop is taken from the larger \(600\), so it removes more than the \(20\%\) that was added to the original \(500\). The fund has \(\boxed{480}\) dollars left.

blocks.x: This lesson applies percent-as-fraction, percent of a number, and percent change to word problems. Four moves: read (each percent has its own whole), translate, solve, check that size and direction make sense.
A hoodie costs \(\$48\) and the local sales tax is \(7.5\%\). How many dollars is the sales tax alone (not the total)?
hints: The tax is just \(7.5\%\) OF the price, exactly the kind of "percent of a number" you did in 9.2. The whole is the 48 dollar price and the rate is \(7.5\%\), so the tax is \(7.5\%\) of 48. Be careful, the question wants the tax by itself, not the 48 plus the tax.
Turn the percent into a decimal before multiplying. Since \(7.5\% = 0.075\), the tax equals \(0.075 \times 48\).
blocks.sol: Walk the wording one step at a time. The price, the whole, is 48 dollars, and the rate is \(7.5\%\). The tax is a straight percent of a number, so the part equals the rate times the whole. First rewrite the percent as a decimal, \(7.5\% = 0.075\). Then the tax is $$0.075 \times 48 = 3.6.$$ So the tax adds 3.6 dollars. Now sanity check the size. A sales tax should be a small slice of the price, well under a tenth of it here, and definitely never bigger than the price itself. A tenth of 48 would be 4.8 dollars, and 3.6 dollars is a bit less than that, which fits \(7.5\%\) being a little under \(10\%\). The tax alone is \(\boxed{3.6}\) dollars.

blocks.x: Four friends have an \(\$80\) dinner bill and agree on an \(18\%\) tip. What is the total amount they pay, food plus tip, in dollars?
hints: You are paying for the whole meal plus a little extra, so think in one multiplier. You owe all \(100\%\) of the food and another \(18\%\) on top, which is \(118\%\) of the food. Turn \(118\%\) into a decimal multiplier and you only have to do one multiplication.
Multiply the food, 80 dollars, by \(1.18\). If you would rather build it in two pieces, find the tip with \(0.18 \times 80\) first, then add that to 80.
blocks.sol: They pay for the full food bill and then add a tip on top, so the total is the whole food plus a bit more. Paying all \(100\%\) of the food and another \(18\%\) is \(118\%\) of the food, which as a multiplier is \(1.18\). So the total is the food times that one number. $$80 \times 1.18 = 94.40$$ You can confirm it the slow way by building the tip separately. The tip is \(0.18 \times 80 = 14.40\), and adding it back gives \(80 + 14.40 = 94.40\), the same total. As a make-sense check, a tip always pushes the bill a little above the food, never below, and 94.40 dollars sits just above 80 dollars by about 14 dollars, exactly what an \(18\%\) tip should feel like. The total they pay is \(\boxed{94.40}\) dollars.

blocks.x: Mira bought a kettle for \(\$180\) and marks it up \(35\%\) of cost. What is the selling price of the kettle in dollars?
hints: A markup works exactly like a tax. The selling price is the whole cost plus another \(35\%\) of that same cost, so it is \(100\% + 35\% = 135\%\) of the cost. Turn that percent into a single multiplier.
\(135\%\) as a decimal is \(1.35\), so the selling price is \(180 \times 1.35\). Multiply that out.
blocks.sol: The cost is the whole, 180 dollars, and the markup adds \(35\%\) of that cost on top. Keeping the full cost and adding another \(35\%\) means the selling price is \(100\% + 35\% = 135\%\) of the cost. As a multiplier that is \(1.35\), so $$\text{selling price} = 180 \times 1.35 = 243.$$ Sanity check. A markup only adds to the cost, so the price should land above 180 dollars, and 243 dollars is comfortably above it. You can also see the pieces fit. The markup alone is \(0.35 \times 180 = 63\) dollars, and \(180 + 63 = 243\) dollars, matching the multiplier method. The selling price is \(\boxed{243}\) dollars.

blocks.cap: Stacking a discount and a tax is just chaining two multipliers, one that pulls below \(1\) and one that pushes above \(1\). Here \(20\%\) off is \(\times\,0.80\) and \(5\%\) tax is \(\times\,1.05\), so 120 dollars becomes \(120 \times 0.80 \times 1.05 = 100.80\) dollars. Because \(0.80 \times 1.05 = 1.05 \times 0.80\), the net effect is the same product whichever order you run the gates in, and the price still ends at 100.80 dollars.

blocks.x: A longboard is tagged at \(\$240\). The shop takes \(25\%\) off, then the register adds \(6\%\) tax on the reduced price. What is the final price paid in dollars?
hints: Handle the two moves one at a time, and notice each one is just a multiplier. Taking \(25\%\) off keeps the \(75\%\) that survives, so that step is \(\times 0.75\). Adding \(6\%\) tax keeps the whole price and piles \(6\%\) on top, so that step is \(\times 1.06\).

Chain the two multipliers in a row rather than doing each as a separate add or subtract. The final price is \(240 \times 0.75 \times 1.06\). Work left to right, \(240 \times 0.75\) first, then multiply that result by \(1.06\).
blocks.sol: Translate each move into a multiplier. A \(25\%\) discount removes a quarter and leaves the surviving \(100\% - 25\% = 75\%\), so that step is \(\times 0.75\). A \(6\%\) tax keeps the full price and adds \(6\%\) on top, which is \(100\% + 6\% = 106\%\), so that step is \(\times 1.06\). Stack them in order. First the discount, $$240 \times 0.75 = 180,$$ which is the price on the shelf after the cut. Then the tax rides on that reduced \(180\), $$180 \times 1.06 = 190.80.$$ Done in one chained product that is \(240 \times 0.75 \times 1.06 = 190.80\). Here is a tidy surprise. If the register had taxed first and discounted second, \(240 \times 1.06 \times 0.75\), the answer would be the exact same \(190.80\), because multiplication does not care about order. The two wholes look different along the way, yet the chained multipliers settle it either road. Now the make-sense check. The \(25\%\) cut should pull the price well below \(240\), and it does, down to \(180\). The small \(6\%\) tax nudges it back up by \(0.06 \times 180 = 10.80\), landing at \(190.80\), still comfortably under \(240\) because the bigger discount outweighs the smaller tax. The final price paid is \(\boxed{190.80}\) dollars.

blocks.x: Every percent change is one multiplier, and stacked changes chain. A tax, tip, or markup keeps the whole price and piles the rate on top, so it is a single $\times(1+\text{rate})$, meaning a $9\%$ tax is just $\times 1.09$. A discount keeps only what survives, $\times(1-\text{rate})$, so $25\%$ off is $\times 0.75$. For the added piece by itself use $\text{part}=\text{rate}\times\text{price}$, but for the new total use the one-move multiplier. Stack several moves and they multiply in a row, $\text{price}\times(1-d)\times(1+t)$, and since multiplication has no favourite order, taxing first or discounting first lands on the exact same price. A total with tax comes out larger, a sale price smaller, so check the direction.
Devon earns a \(12\%\) commission and sold \(\$1{,}450\) in glassware on Sunday. How many dollars does Devon earn in commission that day?
hints: Commission is a percent OF the sale, so this is a plain "percent of a number" problem. The whole is the \(1{,}450\) dollar sale and the rate is \(12\%\), so translate the words into \(0.12 \times 1{,}450\).
Turn \(12\%\) into the decimal \(0.12\), then multiply by \(1{,}450\). A quick way is to find \(10\%\) of the sale, which is \(145\), then add another \(2\%\), which is \(29\), and combine them.
blocks.sol: A commission is a percent of the sale, which is exactly a "percent of a number." Here the whole is the \(1{,}450\) dollar sale and the rate is \(12\%\), so I write \(12\%\) as the decimal \(0.12\) and multiply, $$0.12 \times 1{,}450 = 174.$$ A handy split confirms it. Ten percent of \(1{,}450\) is \(145\), and \(2\%\) is \(29\), and \(145 + 29 = 174\). Now the sanity check. A \(12\%\) commission should be a modest slice of the sale, a touch more than a tenth, and \(174\) sits just above \(145\), which is the \(10\%\) mark, so it is the small share we expected and nowhere near the whole \(1{,}450\). Devon earns a commission of \(\boxed{174}\) dollars.

blocks.x: Naomi earns \(8.5\%\) commission and received \(\$510\) on one telescope sale. What was the sale price of that telescope in dollars?
hints: Name the unknown the sale price and translate the sentence straight across. Her commission is \(8.5\%\) of the sale price, and that commission equals \(510\). So \(510\) is the part, \(8.5\%\) is the rate, and the sale price is the whole you are hunting for. As an equation that reads \(510 = 0.085 \times \text{sale}\).

You are finding the whole from a part, exactly like in 9.2, so undo the multiplication by dividing. Convert \(8.5\%\) to the decimal \(0.085\), then compute \(510 \div 0.085\). The answer should come out much bigger than \(510\), since the commission is only a thin slice of the full price.
blocks.sol: Translate the words one piece at a time. The commission is \(8.5\%\) of the sale price, and that commission is \(510\) dollars, so $$510 = 0.085 \times \text{sale}.$$ This is a find-the-whole-from-a-part problem, so divide both sides by the rate \(0.085\), $$\text{sale} = \frac{510}{0.085} = 6000.$$ Now the sanity check, which really matters here. The sale price of \(6000\) dollars is far larger than the \(510\) dollar commission, which is exactly what we expect since the commission is only a small slice of the full price. And working forward, \(8.5\%\) of \(6000\) is \(0.085 \times 6000 = 510\), the very commission she was paid. The telescope sold for \(\boxed{6000}\) dollars.

blocks.x: Part $=\text{rate}\times\text{whole}$, and running it backward finds the whole. A commission, a tax, a tip: each is a rate times some whole, so to get the part you multiply, $\text{part}=\text{rate}\times\text{whole}$. When instead you know the part and want the whole it came from, undo the multiply by dividing, $\text{whole}=\text{part}\div\text{rate}$. Match the part with its own rate, and never take the rate of the part. Dividing by a small rate makes the whole grow, so if your whole comes out smaller than the part, you divided the wrong way.

A small arrow points down to the in-favor slice with the note its share equals 208 over 320. Another small arrow points down to the against slice with the note its share equals 112 over 320.
