blocks.cap: A two-way split divides one whole into two groups. Here \(320\) votes split into \(208\) in favor and \(112\) against, and the gold slice fills its share of the bar. Each group's percent is its own count over the total of everyone, so in favor is \(\tfrac{208}{320} = 0.65 = 65\%\) and against is \(\tfrac{112}{320} = 0.35 = 35\%\). The two shares always add to the whole, since \(65\% + 35\% = 100\%\).

blocks.x: Maple Ridge Middle School cast \(320\) ballots on a schedule plan. Of those, \(208\) voted in favor. What percent of the votes were in favor of the plan?
hints: Decide what is the part and what is the whole. The question asks about the votes in favor, so the part is the 208 in favor and the whole is all 320 votes that were cast. To find what percent one number is of another, set up the part over the whole and turn it into a percent.
Compute \(\frac{208}{320}\times 100\). Dividing 208 by 320 gives \(0.65\), and multiplying by 100 turns that decimal into a percent.
blocks.sol: The question asks what percent of the votes were in favor, so the part is the 208 votes in favor and the whole is the 320 votes cast. Finding what percent one number is of another means writing the part over the whole and scaling to a percent, so the setup is $$\frac{208}{320}\times 100.$$ First divide to get \(\frac{208}{320}=0.65\), then multiply by 100 to get \(65\), so \(65\%\) of the votes were in favor. As a make-sense check, 208 is a little under two-thirds of 320, and two-thirds is about \(67\%\), so a result in the mid-sixties is right where it should be. There is a second free check too. The against votes are \(320-208=112\), and \(\frac{112}{320}\times 100 = 35\), so the against share is \(35\%\). The two shares add to \(65\% + 35\% = 100\%\), exactly as they must when every ballot is either for or against. The percent who voted in favor is \(\boxed{65}\).

blocks.x: A \(40\)L barrel of mango cooler is \(25\%\) juice. The owner stirs in \(10\) extra liters of plain water, adding no juice. What percent of the new barrel is real mango juice?
hints: The amount of mango juice never changes, only water is added. First find how many liters of juice are in the barrel, that is \(25\%\) of 40, and then find the new total number of liters after the 10 liters of water go in.
The new concentration is the juice amount divided by the new total, turned into a percent. Compute \(\frac{10}{50}\times 100\). Since you only added water, expect a number below \(25\%\).
blocks.sol: First pin down the juice, since that is the part that stays fixed. The juice is \(25\%\) of 40 liters, so \(0.25\times 40 = 10\) liters of mango juice. Adding water does not change that, the barrel still holds 10 liters of juice afterward. Next find the new total. Pouring in 10 liters of water gives \(40 + 10 = 50\) liters in all. Now the new concentration is the juice over the new total, written as a percent. That is \(\frac{10}{50}\times 100 = 20\), so the barrel is \(20\%\) juice. Sanity check the direction. You added only water and no juice, so the drink must get weaker, and \(20\%\) is indeed lower than the original \(25\%\). The dilution makes sense, so the percent of the barrel that is real mango juice is \(\boxed{20}\).

blocks.x: At a beekeeping challenge, \(15\%\) of members did not find the queen in time, and that came to exactly \(9\) members. How many people took the challenge in all?
hints: The 9 who missed the queen are not the whole club, they are the slice that failed. Translate the sentence into math, those 9 people are \(15\%\) of the full group, so \(0.15\) times the group size equals 9. The group size is the whole you are hunting for, the base.
You know the part is 9 and its rate is \(15\%\), and you want the base. Finding a whole from a part means you divide, so compute \(9 \div 0.15\).
blocks.sol: Start by reading carefully what the \(15\%\) describes. It describes the members who tried and did not find the queen, and there were 9 of those. So the 9 are the part, and \(15\%\) is the rate that part makes of the whole club. The whole club is the unknown base. Write that as \(0.15 \times (\text{group size}) = 9\). To find a whole from a part you divide the part by its rate, so the group size is \(9 \div 0.15\). Dividing, \(9 \div 0.15 = 60\). Now check that this makes sense. Only \(15\%\) of the group failed, a small slice, so the whole group should be much bigger than 9, and 60 is comfortably larger. Confirm it by going forward, \(0.15 \times 60 = 9\), exactly the 9 who missed the queen, which leaves \(60 - 9 = 51\) members who found her. Everything lines up, so the number of people who took the challenge is \(\boxed{60}\).

blocks.x: In a two-way split the two shares finish each other off. When everyone falls into one of two groups, the percent for each group is its own count over the total of everyone, and the two shares add to $100\%$, so knowing one hands you the other by subtracting. To turn a count into a percent, write $\frac{\text{count}}{\text{total}}\times 100$. And if you know a count and the percent it makes of the whole, recover the whole by dividing, $\text{whole}=\text{count}\div\text{rate}$, the same find-the-base move. Pair each count with its matching rate, and remember the whole is bigger than any one slice.

<svg viewBox="0 0 560 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="400 muffins shrink by three rushes: times 0.75 to 300, times 0.80 to 240, times 0.50 to 120."> <text x="34" y="26" font-family="Inter" font-size="12.5" font-weight="700" letter-spacing="1.3" fill="#2f6fe0">EACH CUT IS TAKEN OFF WHAT IS LEFT</text> <!-- bars: 400,300,240,120 scale 0.9 px per muffin --> <g font-family="Inter"> <rect x="60" y="44" width="360" height="34" rx="7" fill="#cfe0fa" stroke="#cbd7ea"/> <text x="72" y="66" font-size="14" font-weight="700" fill="#2257c5">400 muffins</text> <rect x="60" y="116" width="270" height="34" rx="7" fill="#cfe0fa" stroke="#cbd7ea"/> <text x="72" y="138" font-size="14" font-weight="700" fill="#2257c5">300</text> <rect x="60" y="188" width="216" height="34" rx="7" fill="#cfe0fa" stroke="#cbd7ea"/> <text x="72" y="210" font-size="14" font-weight="700" fill="#2257c5">240</text> <rect x="60" y="260" width="108" height="34" rx="7" fill="#fcd76a" stroke="#e0a52a" stroke-width="1.5"/> <text x="72" y="282" font-size="14" font-weight="800" fill="#8a5a08">120</text> </g> <!-- arrows + keep labels --> <g stroke="#2f6fe0" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round"> <line x1="440" y1="61" x2="440" y2="133"/><line x1="440" y1="133" x2="350" y2="133"/> <line x1="440" y1="133" x2="440" y2="205"/><line x1="440" y1="205" x2="296" y2="205"/> <line x1="440" y1="205" x2="440" y2="277"/><line x1="440" y1="277" x2="188" y2="277"/> </g> <g fill="#2f6fe0"><path d="M 356 127 l -8 6 l 8 6 z"/><path d="M 302 199 l -8 6 l 8 6 z"/><path d="M 194 271 l -8 6 l 8 6 z"/></g> <g font-family="Inter" font-size="12.5" font-weight="700" fill="#2257c5"> <text x="452" y="100">×0.75 (sell 25%)</text> <text x="452" y="172">×0.80 (sell 20%)</text> <text x="452" y="244">×0.50 (sell 50%)</text> </g> </svg>
blocks.cap: Each rush sells a percent of what is left, not of the original 400. Keeping \(0.75\), then \(0.80\), then \(0.50\) of each remainder gives \(400\times0.75\times0.80\times0.50 = 120\) muffins.

blocks.x: A warehouse has \(720\) boxes. Monday ships \(25\%\). Tuesday ships \(40\%\) of what remains. Wednesday ships \(50\%\) of what is left then. How many boxes remain after Wednesday's shipment?
hints: Each percent is taken of what is LEFT at that moment, not of the starting 720. So instead of tracking what ships, track the fraction that stays. If \(25\%\) ships on Monday, then \(75\%\) stays. If \(40\%\) of the rest ships on Tuesday, then \(60\%\) of the rest stays. Turn each step into the leftover multiplier.

Multiply the leftover fractions one after another onto 720. That is \(720 \times 0.75\) for Monday, then times \(0.60\) for Tuesday, then times \(0.50\) for Wednesday. The question wants the boxes that REMAIN after Wednesday, not the boxes that were shipped, so the final product is your answer.
blocks.sol: The trick is that every percent is taken of the current pile, so it is cleaner to follow what stays than what leaves. Monday ships \(25\%\), so \(100\% - 25\% = 75\%\) stays, a multiplier of \(0.75\). Tuesday ships \(40\%\) of the remaining boxes, so \(60\%\) of them stay, a multiplier of \(0.60\). Wednesday ships \(50\%\) of what is left, so \(50\%\) stays, a multiplier of \(0.50\). Chain the leftover fractions onto the starting 720. $$720 \times 0.75 \times 0.60 \times 0.50$$ Work it left to right. After Monday \(720 \times 0.75 = 540\) boxes remain. After Tuesday \(540 \times 0.60 = 324\) boxes remain. After Wednesday \(324 \times 0.50 = 162\) boxes remain. Sanity check, the pile shrinks every single day, from 720 down to 540 down to 324 down to 162, which is exactly what should happen when boxes only leave. And 162 is a clean whole number of boxes, no fractions of a box, so it makes sense. The question asks how many remain after Wednesday, not how many were shipped, so the answer is the final leftover. \(\boxed{162}\)

blocks.x: When each step takes a percent of what is LEFT, multiply the leftover fractions. A step that acts on what remains works on the smaller pile, never the original, so track what survives: selling $40\%$ leaves $60\%$, then giving away $25\%$ of that leaves $75\%$ of it. The final amount is the start times every leftover fraction multiplied together, like $\text{start}\times 0.60\times 0.75$. Do not add up the percents removed and subtract once, because each later step chips at a pile that already shrank.
The per mille sign ‰ means "per thousand", an extra circle on the percent sign. \(25‰ = 2.5\%\). Sea water runs near \(35‰\) salt. Some countries express blood alcohol limits in per mille.
The Maple Hollow Otters play a \(25\)-game season. After \(13\) games they have \(7\) wins. The coach targets a \(60\%\) win rate for the whole season. How many of the remaining games must they win?
hints: First find how many wins the goal needs across the whole season. The target is \(60\%\) of all 25 games, so translate "60 percent of 25" into multiplication to get the total number of wins the Otters need by the end.
They already have 7 of those wins banked. Subtract to see how many more wins they still need, and notice that those extra wins all have to come out of the games that remain.
blocks.sol: Start by translating the goal. The Otters want to win \(60\%\) of all 25 games, and "60 percent of 25" means multiply, \(0.60 \times 25 = 15\). So the season goal is 15 wins in total. They have already won 7, so the number of wins still needed is \(15 - 7 = 8\). Those wins must come from the games that are left, and the games left are \(25 - 13 = 12\). So the Otters need 8 wins out of their final 12 games. Now run a make-sense check. The answer should be a whole number that is at least 0 and no more than the 12 games remaining, and 8 sits comfortably in that range. To be sure, suppose they win exactly 8 of the last 12. Their season total becomes \(7 + 8 = 15\) wins out of 25, and \(\tfrac{15}{25} = \tfrac{3}{5} = 60\%\), exactly the target. The Otters must win \(\boxed{8}\) of their remaining games.

blocks.x: A keyboard is \(25\%\) off, then \(8\%\) tax is added to the reduced price. Devansh pays \(\$162\). What was the original listed price in dollars?
hints: Run the story forward first to find the chain. Taking 25 percent off multiplies the listed price by \(0.75\), and adding 8 percent tax multiplies by \(1.08\). So the listed price times \(0.75\) times \(1.08\) equals the 162 he paid. Combine the two multipliers into one number before you do anything else.

Since \(0.75 \times 1.08 = 0.81\), the whole deal is just listed price times \(0.81 = 162\). You are given the result and want the starting number, so undo the multiply by dividing. Compute \(162 \div 0.81\).
blocks.sol: Translate the story into multipliers, one step at a time. Marking the keyboard 25 percent off means Devansh pays \(100\% - 25\% = 75\%\) of the listed price, which is the multiplier \(0.75\). Then 8 percent tax is added on the reduced price, and adding 8 percent means multiplying by \(1 + 0.08 = 1.08\). Read forward, the listed price runs through both steps in a row, so $$\text{listed} \times 0.75 \times 1.08 = 162.$$ Collapse the two multipliers into one, \(0.75 \times 1.08 = 0.81\), which leaves $$\text{listed} \times 0.81 = 162.$$ Now run the chain backward. We know the ending total and want the starting price, so undo the multiplication by dividing, $$\text{listed} = \frac{162}{0.81} = 200.$$ Make sense check, run it forward again. The keyboard was listed at 200 dollars, a quarter off brings it to \(200 \times 0.75 = 150\) dollars, and 8 percent tax on 150 is \(150 \times 0.08 = 12\) dollars, for a total of \(150 + 12 = 162\) dollars, exactly what he paid. And the answer should feel right, the original 200 is larger than the 162 he paid, which makes sense because a 25 percent cut followed by only a small 8 percent tax nets out below the full price. The original listed price was \(\boxed{200}\) dollars.

review.x: A kettle sells for \(\$60\) and a \(5\%\) sales tax is added. How many dollars is the sales tax alone?
hints: Sales tax is a percent OF the price. Here that means \(5\%\) of 60 dollars, and the question wants just that tax, not the price plus the tax.
Convert \(5\%\) to \(0.05\) and multiply by 60. Stop there, since the question asks for the tax amount only.
review.sol:
The sales tax is \(5\%\) of the price, so I take a percent of a number. Rewrite \(5\%\) as a decimal, \(5\% = 0.05\), and multiply by the 60 dollar price.

$$0.05 \times 60 = 3$$

The tax is 3 dollars. The question asks for the tax alone, so I stop here and do not add it to the price.

Sanity check. \(5\%\) is one twentieth, and one twentieth of 60 is \(60 \div 20 = 3\), which matches. A 3 dollar tax on a 60 dollar kettle is small, just as a \(5\%\) rate should be. The total with tax would be 63 dollars, but that is not what was asked.

So the sales tax alone is \(\boxed{3}\) dollars.

review.x: A ukulele is listed at \(\$150\) before \(6\%\) sales tax. What is the total amount you pay including the tax?
hints: The total is the sticker price plus the tax. Instead of finding the tax and adding it separately, you can scale the price up in one move with a multiplier.
Adding \(6\%\) on top of the whole price means multiplying by \(1 + 0.06 = 1.06\). So work out \(150 \times 1.06\).
review.sol: The price is 150 dollars and the tax adds \(6\%\) of that price on top. Adding a percent means the multiplier is \(1 + 0.06 = 1.06\), so the total paid is $$150 \times 1.06 = 159.$$ As a sanity check, find the tax on its own. \(6\%\) of 150 is \(0.06 \times 150 = 9\) dollars, and \(150 + 9 = 159\), which matches. The total sits just above the sticker price, exactly as a small tax should make it. So you pay \(\boxed{159}\) dollars.

review.x: Naomi's grooming bill is \(\$40\) and she adds a \(15\%\) tip. How many dollars does Naomi pay in all?
hints: Adding a \(15\%\) tip means Naomi pays the whole \(100\%\) of the bill plus another \(15\%\) of it. As a single multiplier that is \(1 + 0.15\).
Multiply the \(40\) dollar bill by \(1.15\) to get the total, or find \(15\%\) of \(40\) first and then add it back onto the \(40\).
review.sol: A tip is a percent added on, so Naomi pays \(100\%\) of the bill plus another \(15\%\). That turns the bill into the multiplier \(1 + \tfrac{15}{100} = 1.15\). Apply it to the \(40\) dollar bill. $$40 \times 1.15 = 46$$ You can check this the long way. The tip alone is \(15\%\) of \(40\), which is \(0.15 \times 40 = 6\) dollars, and \(40 + 6 = 46\), which matches. Since a tip only adds a little to the bill, a total of \(46\) sitting just above \(40\) makes sense. Naomi pays \(\boxed{46}\) dollars in all.

review.x: Mara earns \(5\%\) commission and closed a \(\$2400\) solar job. How much does she earn in commission, in dollars?
hints: Commission is a percent of the sale, so you want \(5\%\) of the \(\$2400\) job. Turn the percent into a decimal first, \(5\% = 0.05\).
Multiply the sale price by the decimal, \(0.05 \times 2400\), and the result is the commission in dollars.
review.sol: Commission means a percent of the sale, here \(5\%\) of the \(\$2400\) job. Writing the percent as a decimal, \(5\% = \tfrac{5}{100} = 0.05\), so the commission is $$0.05 \times 2400 = 120.$$ A quick sanity check makes this feel right. Ten percent of \(2400\) is \(240\), and \(5\%\) is half of that, which is \(120\), matching. Since \(5\%\) is a small slice of the sale, a commission well under the full \(\$2400\) is exactly what we expect. Mara earns \(\boxed{120}\) dollars in commission.

review.x: Priya earns \(9\%\) commission and received \(\$630\) on one kayak sale. What was the sale price of that kayak in dollars?
hints: Her 630 dollars is \(9\%\) of the kayak's price, so 630 is a part and the full price is the whole you are missing. This is a find-the-whole problem, not a take-a-percent problem.
Write \(0.09 \times \text{price} = 630\), then undo the multiplication by dividing both sides by \(0.09\).
review.sol: The commission is a percent of the sale price, so \(9\%\) of the price equals 630 dollars. Turn the percent into a multiplier and write the relationship as a sentence in math, \(0.09 \times \text{price} = 630\). The price is the whole I am missing, so I divide the part by the rate, $$\text{price} = \frac{630}{0.09} = 7000.$$ Sanity check, take \(9\%\) of 7000 to make sure it lands back on the commission, \(0.09 \times 7000 = 630\), which matches. A 630 dollar commission also feels right for a kayak that costs several thousand dollars, since the commission should be a small slice of a much larger price. The sale price was \(\boxed{7000}\) dollars.

review.x: A garden center pays \(\$90\) for a planter and marks it up \(40\%\). What is the shelf price in dollars?
hints: A markup is a percent increase on the cost the store paid. A \(40\%\) markup means the shelf price is the full cost plus another \(40\%\) of it, which as a multiplier is \(1 + 0.40\).
Multiply the \(90\) dollar cost by the multiplier \(1.40\) to land on the shelf price.
review.sol: A markup is a percent increase laid on top of what the store paid, so the shelf price is \(100\%\) of the cost plus another \(40\%\). That multiplier is \(1 + \tfrac{40}{100} = 1.40\), so the shelf price is $$90 \times 1.40 = 126.$$ You can check this the slow way. \(40\%\) of \(90\) is \(0.40 \times 90 = 36\), the markup in dollars, and \(90 + 36 = 126\), which matches. A quick sanity check fits too. The price went up but not even doubled, and \(126\) sits a comfortable \(36\) dollars above the \(90\) the store paid, exactly the kind of bump a \(40\%\) markup should give. The shelf price is \(\boxed{126}\) dollars.

review.x: A peacoat is priced at \(\$220\) and marked \(15\%\) off. What is the discounted price Devang pays in dollars?
hints: A 15 percent discount means you stop paying 15 percent of the price, so you still pay the other part. What percent of the original price do you actually hand over?
Paying 85 percent of the price is the same as multiplying by \(0.85\). Work out \(220 \times 0.85\).
review.sol: A discount takes a percent off, so the price drops by \(15\%\) and Devang pays the rest. The part he still pays is \(100\% - 15\% = 85\%\) of the listed price, which is the multiplier \(0.85\). Multiply the price by that multiplier. $$220 \times 0.85 = 187$$ Sanity check. The discount itself is \(220 \times 0.15 = 33\) dollars, and \(220 - 33 = 187\), which agrees. The sale price should land below 220 but not too far below for a small \(15\%\) cut, and 187 fits comfortably. So the discounted price he pays is \(\boxed{187}\) dollars.

review.x: A desk lamp is priced at \(\$250\). The shop takes \(10\%\) off, then rings up \(8\%\) tax on the reduced price. What is the final price paid in dollars?
hints: Handle the steps in order. A \(10\%\) discount keeps \(100\% - 10\% = 90\%\) of the price, so multiply by \(0.90\). Then \(8\%\) tax adds onto that result, which is multiplying by \(1.08\).
You can chain the two multipliers into one calculation, \(250 \times 0.90 \times 1.08\). Find the discounted price first, then apply the tax multiplier to it.
review.sol: Take the two changes one at a time. A \(10\%\) discount means the lamp keeps \(100\% - 10\% = 90\%\) of its price, so the discount multiplier is \(1 - 0.10 = 0.90\). Applying it gives the reduced price. $$250 \times 0.90 = 225$$ Now the \(8\%\) tax is added on that reduced \(225\), which is multiplying by \(1 + 0.08 = 1.08\). $$225 \times 1.08 = 243$$ You can also chain both multipliers in one shot, \(250 \times 0.90 \times 1.08 = 243\). Quick sanity check, the discount knocks off \(25\) dollars to reach \(225\), and \(8\%\) of \(225\) is \(18\) dollars of tax, so \(225 + 18 = 243\), which matches. The final price paid is \(\boxed{243}\) dollars.

review.x: A lending library has \(84\) books, and \(21\) were checked out on the weekend. What percent of the library's books were checked out?
hints: "What percent of the whole" means you compare the part to the whole. Here the whole is the full shelf of 84 books, and the part is the 21 that were checked out.
Write the comparison as a fraction \(\frac{21}{84}\), then multiply by \(100\) to turn it into a percent.
review.sol: The question asks what percent of the whole collection was checked out, so the whole is all 84 books and the part is the 21 that left the shelves. To find what percent one number is of another, divide the part by the whole and multiply by 100. That gives $$\frac{21}{84}\times 100.$$ The fraction \(\frac{21}{84}\) simplifies, since \(21\) divides into \(84\) exactly 4 times, so \(\frac{21}{84}=\frac{1}{4}\). Then \(\frac{1}{4}\times 100 = 25\), so \(25\%\) of the books were checked out. As a sanity check, a quarter of 84 is \(21\), which matches the number checked out, and \(25\%\) feels right because 21 is well under half of 84. The answer is \(\boxed{25}\).

review.x: A robotics club of \(200\) members voted on practice time. Exactly \(140\) voted for mornings. What percent voted for evenings? Give the number only.
hints: Only two choices were on the table, so the evening voters are everyone who did not pick mornings. Start by finding that count with \(200 - 140\).
To find what percent the evening voters are of the club, divide their count by the WHOLE membership of \(200\), then multiply by \(100\).
review.sol: There are only two choices, so the evening voters are the members left over after the \(140\) morning voters. That count is \(200 - 140 = 60\). To find what percent this is of the whole club, divide by the total membership, not by the other side. $$\frac{60}{200} = 0.30$$ As a percent that is \(0.30 \times 100 = 30\%\). You can sanity-check it against the morning side, which is \(\tfrac{140}{200} = 70\%\), and \(70\% + 30\% = 100\%\) covers everyone exactly. So the percent who voted for evenings is \(\boxed{30}\).

review.x: Exactly \(20\%\) of people who sat a certification exam did not pass, and that group numbered \(12\). How many people took the exam in all?
hints: The \(12\) people who did not pass are not the whole group, they are the \(20\%\) slice of it. So \(12\) is \(20\%\) of the number you want, which means \(0.20 \times \text{total} = 12\).
To undo the multiplier, divide the part by its fraction. Work out \(12 \div 0.20\), which is the same as asking what number has \(12\) as its fifth.
review.sol: The \(12\) people who did not pass are the \(20\%\) of the whole group that fell short, so they are a part, not the total. Writing \(20\%\) as the decimal \(0.20\), this says $$0.20 \times \text{total} = 12.$$ To find the total, divide the part by the fraction it represents rather than multiplying. $$\text{total} = \frac{12}{0.20} = 60.$$ As a check, take \(20\%\) of \(60\) and see if you land back on \(12\). Since \(0.20 \times 60 = 12\), the count holds, and the other \(80\%\) who passed comes out to \(48\), which fits because \(48 + 12 = 60\). So \(\boxed{60}\) people took the exam.

review.x: A crew prints \(600\) flyers. They hand out \(20\%\) to passersby. Then rain ruins \(25\%\) of the flyers still in the box. How many flyers does the crew have left?
hints: Each percent is taken from what is still in the box at that moment, not from the original 600. After handing out \(20\%\), what fraction of the stack is left? Then the rain ruins \(25\%\) of that smaller amount.

Handing out \(20\%\) leaves \(80\%\), so \(600 \times 0.80\) flyers remain. Then losing \(25\%\) of those keeps \(75\%\), so multiply by \(0.75\). Multiplying the leftover fractions gives the count that survives both steps.
review.sol:
This is a percent-of-the-remaining chain, where each step takes a percent of whatever is left at that point, so I multiply the leftover fractions instead of subtracting from the original 600.

Start with 600 flyers. Handing out \(20\%\) means \(20\%\) leave and \(80\%\) stay, so the box still holds
$$600 \times 0.80 = 480$$
flyers after the morning.

The rain then ruins \(25\%\) of those 480, so \(75\%\) survive. The key is that this \(25\%\) is taken from the 480 in the box, not from the original 600.
$$480 \times 0.75 = 360$$

I can do both steps at once by multiplying the leftover fractions, \(600 \times 0.80 \times 0.75 = 360\), which matches.

Sanity check. The crew handed out \(600 - 480 = 120\) flyers in the morning, and the rain ruined \(480 - 360 = 120\). That accounts for \(120 + 120 = 240\) flyers gone, leaving \(600 - 240 = 360\) in the box, so the pieces add back to the original 600. The answer is also smaller than the 480 from after the first step, which makes sense since more were lost. The number of flyers remaining at the end of the day is \(\boxed{360}\).

review.x: An \(80\)L barrel of polishing fluid is \(25\%\) solvent. An apprentice stirs in \(20\) more liters of plain water. What percent of the new mixture is solvent? Give the number only.
hints: Adding plain water does not add any solvent, so first find how many liters of solvent the original barrel held by taking \(25\%\) of \(80\).
Keep that solvent amount fixed, work out the new total after the extra water, then write the solvent as a fraction of the new total and turn it into a percent.
review.sol: Start with what is actually in the barrel. The solvent is \(25\%\) of \(80\) liters, so \(0.25 \times 80 = 20\) liters of solvent, and the other \(60\) liters are water. Pouring in \(20\) more liters of plain water adds no solvent, so the solvent stays at \(20\) liters while the total grows to \(80 + 20 = 100\) liters. The new concentration compares the solvent to the WHOLE new mixture, so divide \(20\) by \(100\). That gives \(\tfrac{20}{100} = 0.20\), and as a percent \(0.20 \times 100 = 20\%\). Sanity check, the mixture got more watery, so its solvent percent should drop below the original \(25\%\), and \(20\%\) is indeed lower. So the new mixture is \(\boxed{20}\) percent solvent.

review.x: The Marlowe Middle quiz bowl team plays a \(28\)-game season. After \(16\) games they have \(9\) wins. They want a \(50\%\) win rate for the whole season. How many of their remaining games must they win?
hints: A \(50\%\) win rate over the whole season means winning half of all \(28\) games. Find that target number of total wins first.
Subtract the wins they already have from the target total. The difference is how many more wins they still need from the remaining games.
review.sol: First find how many total wins a \(50\%\) season needs. Half of all \(28\) games is $$\tfrac{1}{2} \times 28 = 14$$ so the team must finish with \(14\) wins. They have already won \(9\), so the wins they still need are \(14 - 9 = 5\). That is the answer, since the question asks for wins from the remaining games, not the total. Sanity check the count. The team has \(28 - 16 = 12\) games left, so winning \(5\) of them is possible. If they do, their wins come to \(9 + 5 = 14\), and \(\tfrac{14}{28} = \tfrac{1}{2} = 50\%\), exactly the target. The team must win \(\boxed{5}\) of its remaining games.
